1.1: The vision that Isaiah son of Amoz saw — a word over Judah and Jerusalem — in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.
1.2:
Hear, O heavens — listen, O earth!
For Yahweh himself has spoken:
"Sons I raised and nurtured to full strength,
but they have turned against me."
1.3:
An ox knows the man who feeds it;
even a donkey remembers its master's manger —
but Israel knows nothing.
My own people will not see.
1.6:
From the soles of your feet to the top of your head:
not a sound place anywhere —
only welts, bruises, and festering sores,
not pressed clean, not bandaged, not softened with oil.
1.7:
Your land lies desolate; your cities have been burned to the ground.
Foreigners strip your fields bare while you watch —
a wasteland wrecked as only an enemy can wreck it.
1.8:
And the daughter of Zion is abandoned —
a flimsy harvest-hut alone in a vineyard,
a watchman's lean-to in a cucumber patch,
a city that has been ringed and pressed hard.
1.9:
Had Yahweh, Commander of all armies,
not preserved a small surviving seed among us,
we would have been Sodom outright —
not one stone left standing, like Gomorrah.
1.10:
Hear the word of Yahweh, you who rule like Sodom's princes!
Let the teaching of our God reach your ears, you who live like Gomorrah's people!
1.11:
"What do I want with the mass of your sacrifices?"
says Yahweh.
"I am sated with burnt rams
and the grease of well-fattened beasts.
The blood of bulls, the blood of lambs and goats —
I take no delight in any of it."
1.12:
When you come to stand in my presence —
who asked you for this?
Who invited you to wear out my courts with your feet?
1.13:
Bring no more hollow offerings;
your incense is a stench to me.
New moons, sabbaths, called assemblies —
I cannot stomach evil and sacred ceremony held together.
1.14:
Your new-moon ceremonies and your appointed festivals —
I find them repulsive.
They weigh on me like a load I am too tired to carry any longer.
1.15:
When you stretch out your hands to me in prayer,
I will shut my eyes.
When you multiply your prayers, I will not hear.
Your hands are dripping with blood.
1.16:
Wash off the guilt — make yourselves clean!
Get the evil of your actions out of my sight.
Stop doing wrong.
1.17:
Learn to do good.
Seek justice — rein in the oppressor.
Champion the orphan;
stand up and argue the widow's case.
1.18:
"Come now, let us face this together," says Yahweh.
"Even if your sins are as crimson-red as scarlet dye,
they can be made white as snow.
Even if they are stained deep as dyed wool,
they can be made white as fresh fleece."
1.19:
If you are willing and choose to obey,
you will feast on the land's finest —
1.20:
but if you refuse and go on resisting,
the sword will devour you instead.
For these are the very words of Yahweh's own mouth.
1.21:
How the faithful city has become a whore!
Once she was brimming with justice;
righteousness made its home in her —
but now the city belongs to murderers.
1.22:
Your silver has become slag;
your best wine — watered down.
1.24:
Therefore — this is the declaration of the Sovereign,
Yahweh of the heavenly armies, the Mighty One of Israel:
"I will have done with those who oppose me;
I will satisfy myself against my enemies."
1.25:
I will bring my hand down hard upon you —
I will smelt you clean, burning away your slag with caustic lye,
removing every trace of alloy.
1.26:
Then I will give you back your rightful judges —
leaders like those of the former days,
counselors like those of old.
And afterward you will be called the City of Righteousness,
the Faithful City.
1.27:
Zion will be ransomed through justice;
and those within her who return will be restored through righteousness.
1.28:
But rebels and sinners — they will be shattered together;
those who have walked away from Yahweh will be burned out.
1.29:
You will be put to shame for those oak groves you coveted —
confounded by the sacred gardens you made your own.
1.30:
You will be like an oak tree
whose leaves curl and wither away —
like a garden no rain has reached.
1.31:
The strong man will become kindling,
and his deeds the spark that lights him —
both will burn together, and no one will quench the fire.

2.1: A word that Isaiah son of Amoz saw — and it concerned Judah and Jerusalem.
2.2:
In the days yet to come,
the mountain where the LORD's house stands
shall be set as the crown of all mountains —
raised above every hill —
and all the nations will come streaming toward it.
2.3:
And many peoples will be saying to one another,
"Come — let us go up to the mountain of Yahweh,
to the house of the God of Jacob.
Let him instruct us in his ways
so that we may walk his paths."
For the Torah will go out from Zion;
the word of Yahweh from Jerusalem.
2.4:
He will arbitrate between the nations
and settle disputes for peoples far and wide.
Then they will beat their swords into plowshares
and their spears into pruning hooks.
Nation will not raise sword against nation;
war will no longer be a lesson taught.
2.5:
O family of Jacob — come!
Let us walk together in the light of Yahweh.
2.6:
For you have cast off your own people, the house of Jacob —
because they have filled themselves with customs from the east,
dabble in divination like the Philistines,
and strike deals with every foreign nation.
2.7:
Their land is glutted with silver and gold —
treasures without limit;
their land packed with horses —
chariots beyond counting.
2.8:
Their land is choked with idols.
They bow down before the work of their own hands,
before what their own fingers have shaped.
2.10:
Crawl into the rock!
Bury yourself in the dust!
Hide from the terror of Yahweh —
from the blinding splendor of his majesty.
2.12:
For Yahweh, Commander of all armies, has set a day —
a day against everything that has made itself great,
against every towering pride —
and it will all be brought crashing down.
2.13:
Against every cedar of Lebanon, sky-high and proud;
against every oak of Bashan.
2.14:
Against every high mountain;
against every swelling hill.
2.15:
Against every tall tower;
against every fortified wall.
2.16:
Against every ship bound for Tarshish;
against every elegant vessel.
2.18: As for the idols — they will simply cease to be.
2.19:
Then people will scramble into rocky caves
and burrow into holes in the ground —
fleeing Yahweh's terrifying presence,
the blinding glory of his majesty,
when he rises to make the earth tremble.
2.20:
On that day, every person will hurl away
the silver and gold idols they fashioned for themselves —
casting them to the moles,
tossing them to the bats.
2.21:
They will press into rock-clefts,
wedge themselves into the cracks of the crags —
all to flee Yahweh's terrifying presence,
the blinding glory of his majesty,
when he rises to make the earth tremble.
2.22:
Stop trusting in human beings,
who have no more than a breath in their nostrils.
Of what lasting account is such a person?

3.1: Mark this well: the Lord, Yahweh of hosts, is stripping away from Jerusalem and Judah every prop and stay — every last crust of bread and every drop of water.
3.2: the soldier and the warrior, the judge and the prophet, the fortune-teller and the elder,
3.3: the officer of fifty and the man of honour, the shrewd counselor and the master craftsman and the one skilled in spells.
3.4: I will give them children for rulers and make infants their masters.
3.5: Society will unravel — neighbour against neighbour, the young contemptuous of the old, the base person brazen toward the respectable.
3.6: A man will grab his own brother by the sleeve in their father's house and plead: 'At least you own a cloak — be our leader! Rule over this rubble!'
3.7: And even then the brother will swear: 'I have no remedy to offer. There is no bread in my house, no cloak on my back. Do not appoint me over this people.'
3.8: Jerusalem has stumbled; Judah has gone down — because their words and their actions are a direct affront to Yahweh, a defiance of his all-seeing glory.
3.9: Their own faces give them away — guilt worn openly like a badge. They advertise their sin the way Sodom did, without a trace of shame. Woe to them! They have heaped this disaster on their own heads.
3.10: Speak this word to the righteous: all shall be well with them — they will feast on the harvest of what they have sown.
3.11: But woe to the wicked — disaster awaits! Every deed their hands have dealt out will be dealt back to them in full.
3.12: My people — their masters are children; women hold sway over them. O my people, the ones who lead you are leading you to ruin; they have obliterated every right road.
3.13: Yahweh rises to plead his case — he takes his place in the courtroom of history to pronounce sentence on the nations.
3.14: Yahweh himself enters judgment against the elders and rulers of his people: 'You are the ones who have stripped the vineyard bare. The plunder taken from the poor is stockpiled in your mansions.'
3.15: 'How dare you grind my people to powder, how dare you mill the faces of the poor into the dirt?' — this is the verdict of the Lord Yahweh of hosts.
3.16: Yahweh declared: Because the women of Zion carry themselves in arrogance — necks craned, eyes roving with calculated allure, stepping with an affected mince, chiming at every step with their ankle ornaments —
3.17: the Lord will strike their heads with scabs, and Yahweh will strip them bare of every dignity.
3.18: In that day the Lord will rip away the tinkling anklets and the elaborate headdresses and the crescent pendants,
3.19: the drop earrings, the charm bracelets, and the filmy scarves,
3.20: the fine turbans and armlets and broad sashes, the perfume flasks and the good-luck amulets,
3.21: the signet rings and the nose rings,
3.22: the elegant festal gowns and the capes and the shawls and the clutch bags,
3.23: the polished bronze mirrors, the fine linen shifts, the jewelled tiaras, and the flowing veils.
3.24:
Where there was perfume — only rot.
Where there was a jewelled belt — a knotted rope.
Where there was an elaborate coiffure — a shaved head.
Where there was a gorgeous robe — a sack of coarse cloth.
Where there was beauty — a slave's brand.
3.25: Her men will be cut down by the sword; her fighting men will die in combat.
3.26: Her gates will wail in mourning and desolation — and she will sit on the bare earth, stripped and alone.

4.1: So severe will be the slaughter of men that in that day seven women will lay hold of a single man, pleading: 'We will feed ourselves, we will clothe ourselves — only let us carry your name. Take away the shame of our childlessness.'
4.2: Yet in that same day the Branch of Yahweh will emerge — radiant and glorious — and the fruit of the land will be the crown jewel of every Israelite who has come through the fire of judgment.
4.3: Those who survive in Zion, those who remain in Jerusalem — they will be called holy. Every one of them whose name is written in the roll of the living in Jerusalem will bear that sacred designation.
4.4: This will be the hour when the Lord has scoured away the moral filth of Zion's daughters and purged the bloodguilt from Jerusalem's heart — by a spirit of justice that burns like a refining fire.
4.5: Then Yahweh himself will create over every site of Mount Zion and over all her assemblies a cloud by day and a pillar of smoke, a blaze of fire by night — the glory of the Exodus renewed, a canopy of radiant presence spread over everything.
4.6: There will be a pavilion — shade from the scorching heat of day, shelter and refuge against every driving storm and rain.

5.1:
Let me sing a love song for my dear friend —
a song about his vineyard:
My beloved had a vineyard
planted on a richly-fertile slope.
5.2:
He broke the ground, cleared every stone,
planted it with the choicest of vines.
He built a watchtower at its centre,
hewed a winepress into the rock.
He waited for sweet grapes —
but it gave him nothing but bitter, wild fruit.
5.3:
Now then — you who live in Jerusalem, you men of Judah:
render your verdict between me and my vineyard.
5.4:
What more could I have done for this vineyard that I did not do?
Tell me — why, when I looked for a harvest of sweet grapes,
did it return only bitterness?
5.5:
Very well — let me tell you what I will do to my vineyard.
I will tear out its hedge and leave it to be eaten bare.
I will knock down its wall and let it be trampled flat.
5.6:
I will abandon it to desolation —
no pruning, no hoeing, only thorns and briars springing up.
And I will order the very clouds to hold back their rain.
5.7:
The vineyard of Yahweh of hosts is the house of Israel;
the men of Judah are his beloved garden-planting.
He looked for justice — but found only bloodshed.
He looked for righteousness — but heard only screaming.
(The Hebrew makes the irony stark: he hoped for mishpat — received mishpach; for tsedaqah — heard tseaqah.)
5.8:
Woe to those who swallow up house after house,
who absorb field after field until the last boundary stone is gone —
until they are the only ones left, sole lords over the entire land.
5.9:
This is what rang in my ears — the oath of Yahweh of hosts:
'However grand and magnificent those houses may be,
not one of them will have a living soul inside.'
5.10: The land itself will fail them: ten whole acres of vineyard yielding a single gallon of wine, ten bushels of seed producing barely one.
5.11:
Woe to those who are already hunting for liquor at sunrise
and drink their way deep into the night,
until the wine is blazing through them.
5.12:
Their banquets overflow with harp and lute,
tambourine and flute and cup after cup of wine —
but the deeds of Yahweh they cannot be bothered to see,
and what his hands are doing they refuse to consider.
5.13: This is why my people will be marched away into captivity — they refused to know what they needed to know. Their nobility will go hungry; their great crowds will perish of thirst.
5.14:
Therefore Sheol, the realm of the dead, opens its throat wide — swallowing without measure —
and down will go Jerusalem's glittering nobility, her noisy crowds,
her pomp and all her party-revellers.
5.15:
Every man is brought low, every person humbled —
the lofty eyes of the arrogant are pulled to the ground.
5.16:
Yet Yahweh of hosts is lifted high through the act of justice,
and the Holy God, the wholly other One, hallows himself through righteousness.
5.17: In that overturned world, lambs will graze where the grand estates once stood, and the wandering poor will eat among the rubble of the luxurious.
5.18:
Woe to those who harness themselves to evil as if with halter ropes,
who haul sin along behind them like a loaded wagon —
5.19:
— those who taunt: 'If God has a plan, let him get on with it — let him show us!
Let the Holy One of Israel bring his great purpose to pass so we can see it for ourselves!'
5.20:
Woe to those who rename evil as good and good as evil,
who swap darkness for light and light for darkness,
who trade bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter!
5.21:
Woe to those whose only oracle is their own judgment,
whose only wisdom is their own reflection!
5.22:
Woe to those who win medals for drinking wine,
who earn their reputation mixing the strongest brew,
5.23:
— while they let criminals walk free for a fee
and grind the rights of the innocent into the dirt.
5.24:
So then — as fire licks up a field of stubble
and dry grass crumbles in the flame,
their root will go rotten and their flower will blow away as dust.
For they have thrown away the Torah of Yahweh of hosts
and treated the word of the Holy One of Israel with contempt.
5.26:
He raises his battle-standard for a far-off nation
and whistles it in from the ends of the earth —
watch: it is already coming, swift and sure.
5.27:
No fatigue in their ranks, no stumbling, not a man asleep;
every belt tight, every sandal strap intact — they march without rest.
5.28:
Arrows keen-edged, every bow bent back and ready —
hooves that ring on the road like flint,
wheels that blur into a whirlwind.
5.30:
That day they will surge and thunder over the land like a raging sea.
Look at the land — nothing but darkness and dread.
Even the light is swallowed by overhanging clouds.

6.1: In the year King Uzziah died, I saw the Lord — seated on a throne soaring high and lifted up — and the flowing train of his robe filled the entire temple.
6.2: Seraphim attended him — each with six wings: two covering their faces in awe, two veiling their feet in reverence, two bearing them aloft.
6.3:
'Holy, holy, holy is Yahweh of hosts —
the whole earth is filled with his glory!'
They called this to one another, antiphon and echo in the vaulted space.
6.4: At the sound of that holy calling the great door-posts shuddered on their foundations, and the house filled with smoke.
6.5: 'Woe to me — I am undone! I am a man of polluted lips, living in the middle of a people with polluted lips — and yet my own eyes have seen the King, Yahweh of hosts!'
6.6: Then one of the seraphim flew to where I stood, holding in his hand a live coal taken from the altar with iron tongs.
6.7: He pressed it to my mouth and declared: 'This burning coal has touched your lips. Your guilt — lifted. Your sin — covered and cancelled.'
6.8: Then I heard the voice of the Lord — 'Whom shall I send? Who will go on our behalf?' And I said, 'Here I am. Send me.'
6.9: And he said: 'Go. Speak this word to this people: Hear and hear again — but never grasp it. Look and look again — but never see.'
6.10: 'Dull their hearts. Weight their ears with stone. Seal their eyes shut — lest they actually see, and hear, and understand with their hearts, and turn back, and I heal them.'
6.11: 'How long, O Lord?' I asked. And he answered: 'Until every city is a rubble-field with no one left inside, until every house is empty and the whole land is an abandoned wasteland —'
6.12: '— until Yahweh has exiled the people to the far ends of the earth, and the forsaken stretches of land are wide and empty at the centre of what was once home.'
6.13: Even the tenth that survives will pass through fire once more — like a terebinth or oak that is felled, leaving only the stump. But that stump is the holy seed: the remnant that cannot be destroyed, the hidden life through which all that was promised will rise again.

7.1: In the reign of Ahaz — grandson of Uzziah, king of Judah — Rezin of Syria and Pekah son of Remaliah of Israel marched on Jerusalem to besiege it, but they could not take the city.
7.2: Word reached the royal house: Syria has joined forces with Ephraim. At once Ahaz's heart, and the hearts of all his people, shook like forest trees tossing in the wind.
7.3: Yahweh said to Isaiah: 'Go meet Ahaz — take your son Shear-jashub with you — at the end of the channel from the upper reservoir, on the Fuller's Field road.'
7.4: 'Tell him: Hold steady — calm down and do not be afraid. Do not let these two smoldering stumps frighten you, the spent rage of Rezin and Syria and of Remaliah's son.'
7.5: Syria has conspired with Ephraim and Remaliah's son against you, saying:
7.6: 'Let us invade Judah, crack it open, install our puppet — the son of Tabeal — as its king.'
7.7: But the Lord Yahweh says: It will not happen. It will never stand.
7.8: Syria's capital is Damascus; Damascus has only Rezin. And within sixty-five years Ephraim will be so thoroughly broken it will no longer exist as a nation.
7.9: Ephraim's capital is Samaria; Samaria has only Remaliah's son. If you will not trust, you cannot last.
7.11: Ask Yahweh your God for a sign — anything you choose: as deep as the grave below or as high as the heavens above.
7.12: Ahaz replied: 'I will not ask. I refuse to test Yahweh.'
7.13: Isaiah answered: 'Listen to me, house of David. Is it not enough that you exhaust human patience — must you also exhaust the patience of my God?'
7.14: Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign: Look — a young woman is with child and will give birth to a son; she will name him Immanuel, God-with-us.
7.15: He will live on curds and honey until he is old enough to reject what is evil and choose what is good.
7.16: Even before this child knows right from wrong, the land of those two kings you fear will be abandoned by both its rulers.
7.17: Yahweh will bring on you, your people, and your entire dynasty days unlike any since the day Israel broke away from Judah — he will bring the king of Assyria.
7.18: On that day Yahweh will whistle up the flies from Egypt's farthest rivers and summon the bees from Assyria's homeland.
7.19: They will swarm in — covering every rocky gully, every crevice, every thornbush and every open pasture.
7.20: On that day the Lord will hire Assyria as his razor — those people from beyond the Euphrates — and shave Israel completely bare: head, body hair, and beard all gone.
7.21: That day a man will count himself fortunate to keep one young cow and two sheep.
7.22: Those few animals will give enough milk that everyone remaining in the land will live on curds and honey — the diet of a stripped and emptied country.
7.23: On that day every vineyard that once commanded a thousand pieces of silver will be overrun with briers and thorns.
7.24: Hunters with bows will roam what was once farmland, now given over entirely to briers and thorns.
7.25: Every hillside once broken open with a hoe — no one will enter them for fear of the thorns. Those once-tended slopes will be handed over to cattle and trampled by flocks.

8.1: Yahweh told me: 'Get a large tablet and inscribe on it in plain letters: Maher-shalal-hash-baz.'
8.2: I then summoned two reliable witnesses: Uriah the priest and Zechariah son of Jeberechiah.
8.3: I was intimate with the prophetess; she conceived and bore a son. And Yahweh said: 'Name him Maher-shalal-hash-baz — Swift-plunder, Quick-prey.'
8.4: Before this child can say 'Daddy' or 'Mama,' the riches of Damascus and the spoil of Samaria will be hauled away by the king of Assyria.
8.5: Yahweh continued to speak to me:
8.6: Because this people has spurned the gently flowing waters of Siloam and instead delights in Rezin and Remaliah's son,
8.7: therefore the Lord is bringing the Euphrates in full flood against them — the king of Assyria with all his might, surging over every channel and overflowing every bank —
8.8: sweeping through Judah in a torrent, rising until it reaches the throat, and spreading like a great bird's wings over the whole breadth of your land. Immanuel — this is still your land, God-with-us.
8.9:
Band together, you nations — you will still be shattered!
Rally your distant peoples — arm for war and be crushed!
Gird yourselves again — you will be crushed!
8.10:
Plot your strategy — it will fall apart.
Issue your orders — they will never hold.
For God is with us — Immanuel.
8.11: Yahweh gripped me with his hand and warned me: do not walk the path this people walks. He said:
8.14: He will be a sanctuary for those who trust him — but for both houses of Israel he will be a stone people trip over, a rock they fall against, a trap and a snare for the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
8.15: Many will trip on that stone — they will fall and break, be caught and taken.
8.16:
Bind up this testimony;
seal this Torah among my disciples.
8.17:
I will wait on Yahweh,
who has hidden his face from the house of Jacob.
I will watch for him.
8.18:
Look: I and the children Yahweh has given me —
we are signs and wonders in Israel
from Yahweh of hosts who dwells on Mount Zion.
8.19: When people tell you: 'Go consult the mediums and spiritists — those who whisper and mutter their dark words' — ask them: Should a people not seek their God? Why would the living turn to the dead?
8.20:
Back to the Torah! Back to the testimony!
Any word that contradicts this has no light in it at all.
8.21:
They will wander through the land, exhausted and starving;
when the hunger bites, rage will flare up — they will curse their king, curse their God, and look upward —
8.22: and then look to the earth and find nothing but distress, darkness, the gloomy black of anguish — driven at last into an outer dark with no way out.

9.1: Yet the darkness that lay on Zebulun and Naphtali will not remain forever. In the first time Yahweh brought shame on those northern lands, but in the latter time he will honor the coastal road, Transjordan, and Galilee — the country of the nations.
9.2:
The people who walked in darkness
have seen a blazing light;
those who lived in the land of death's shadow —
on them the light has broken through.
9.3:
You have swelled the nation's numbers and multiplied its joy —
they rejoice before you as at harvest time,
as soldiers celebrate when they divide what they have won.
9.4:
For you have shattered the yoke that weighed on Israel's shoulder,
snapped the staff the oppressor wielded against them,
as you broke Midian's power on that ancient day.
9.5:
Every soldier's boot worn in the thunder of battle,
every uniform caked in blood —
all of it will be fuel for the fire,
burned up and gone.
9.6:
For a child is born to us — a son is given!
The government rests on his shoulders.
His name is:
Wonderful Counselor,
Mighty God,
Father of Eternity,
Prince of Peace.
9.7:
His reign will keep expanding, and peace will never stop —
seated on the throne of David,
ruling his kingdom, sustaining it,
upholding it with justice and righteousness
from now and forever.
The zeal of Yahweh of hosts will make it so.
9.8: The Lord dispatched a word against Jacob — it has landed on Israel.
9.9: All the people of Ephraim will know it — Samaria's inhabitants too — though they say with proud, stubborn hearts:
9.10:
Our mud bricks have crumbled — we will rebuild with dressed stone!
The sycamores are down — we will plant cedars in their place!
9.11: So Yahweh has strengthened Rezin's enemies against them and roused their foes to strike —
9.12:
Arameans from the east, Philistines from the west —
they swallow Israel whole.
Yet for all of this, his anger is not spent;
his hand is still raised to strike.
9.13:
Still the people did not turn to the one who was striking them;
they would not seek Yahweh of hosts.
9.14:
So Yahweh severed from Israel in a single day
both head and tail, both towering palm and lowly reed —
9.15:
the honored elder is the head;
the prophet who peddles lies is the tail.
9.16:
The leaders of this people have led them into confusion,
and those they guide are consumed.
9.18:
Wickedness blazes like fire —
it burns through briers and thorns;
it catches in the forest thickets
and billows up in columns of smoke.
9.21:
Manasseh turns on Ephraim, Ephraim turns on Manasseh,
and both together tear at Judah.
Yet for all of this, his anger is not spent;
his hand is still raised to strike.

10.1:
Woe to those who carve injustice into law,
who write exploitation into the statute books,
10.2:
— laws that deny the poor any recourse,
that strip the rights of the afflicted of my people,
that leave widows as easy pickings
and orphans to be plundered.
10.3:
And when the day of reckoning finally arrives —
when ruin sweeps in from the far horizon —
where will you run?
To whom will you cry out for help?
Where will you stash what you have taken?
10.4:
There is nowhere left to go — only to sink down among the prisoners
or fall among the slain.
For all of this, his anger has not turned back;
his hand is raised to strike again.
10.5:
Woe, Assyria — you are the rod of my rage,
the weapon gripped by a hand that is my own indignation!
10.6:
I have sent him against a people who have forsaken me —
ordered him to raid and ravage, to tread the godless underfoot
like mud on a city street.
10.7:
But Assyria sees none of that. He is no one's errand-boy.
Destruction is all he has in mind — nation after nation annihilated.
That is the whole of his agenda.
10.8: He boasts: 'Look at my generals — every one of them is as good as a king!'
10.9:
Calno fell like Carchemish. Hamath like Arpad. Samaria like Damascus.
They were all the same to me.
10.10: My hand has already taken kingdoms whose idol-images were grander than anything Jerusalem or Samaria ever displayed —
10.11: — so shall I not do the same to Jerusalem and all the things she worships?
10.12: When Yahweh has accomplished everything he set out to do on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, he will turn his attention to the king of Assyria — punishing the pride that drove the instrument, the strutting arrogance blazing in those boastful eyes.
10.13:
'I did this by my own strength, my own genius — no one can question my brilliance.
I erased their national borders as easily as drawing a line in sand.
I ransacked their treasuries.
I toppled enthroned kings like a battering ram breaks a gate.'
10.14:
'Their wealth? An unguarded nest — I just reached in.
I swept up the whole earth and no one stirred a wing,
no one opened a beak,
not a sound of resistance.'
10.15:
Does an axe congratulate itself on what the hand achieves?
Does the saw credit itself rather than the arm that draws it?
As if a stick could wave the one who swings it —
as if a staff could carry the man!
10.16:
So the Sovereign Lord — Yahweh of hosts — will send a wasting plague
through the ranks of Assyria's finest troops,
and beneath all that vaunted glory
a fire will ignite and eat its way through.
10.17:
The Light of Israel will turn into a fire;
the Holy One will be a consuming flame —
and in a single day it will devour every thorn and brier
that Assyria has grown.
10.18:
Forest and orchard — his proudest glories —
will be eaten up entirely, soul and body,
until the whole empire fades like a man dying of fever.
10.19:
Whatever trees survive?
So few that a child could jot down every last one of them.
10.20:
In that day the remnant of Israel,
the survivors of Jacob's household,
will stop leaning on the empire that once struck them.
They will lean on Yahweh alone —
the Holy One of Israel — and they will mean it.
10.21:
A remnant will return — Jacob's remnant —
back to the Mighty God.
(El Gibbor: the same divine title given to the promised child in 9:6; the remnant returns to him.)
10.22:
Your people, Israel, may be as countless as grains of sea-sand —
but only a remnant will come back.
The destruction that has been decreed will overflow like a flood;
and it is a flood of righteousness.
10.23:
The Lord Yahweh of hosts has issued his decree —
a destruction inevitable and complete,
sweeping through the whole land.
10.24:
So — hear this word from the Lord Yahweh of hosts:
'My people in Zion, do not be terrorised by Assyria.
Yes, he will strike with his rod, raise his staff against you — exactly as Egypt did.
Do not be afraid.'
10.25:
It will not last long.
In a very little while my indignation will exhaust itself —
and then my anger will wheel around and fall on them.
10.26:
Yahweh of hosts will crack the whip against Assyria —
the same decisive blow as at the rock of Oreb when Midian fell.
His rod will stretch out over the sea,
raised again as it was raised against Egypt.
10.27:
In that day the oppressor's burden will be lifted from your shoulder,
his yoke broken from your neck —
shattered by the anointed one.
10.28: He reaches Aiath. Passes through Migron. Depots his gear at Michmash.
10.29:
The pass is crossed. They bed down at Geba.
Ramah is shaking. Gibeah of Saul — already gone.
10.30:
Gallim's daughter — scream it!
Laishah, hear it!
Anathoth, cry back the warning!
10.31: Madmenah bolts. Gebim's people scatter for their lives.
10.32:
He stops at Nob — one hill away.
From there he raises his fist against Zion's mountain,
the hill of Jerusalem.
10.33:
But watch:
the Sovereign Lord — Yahweh of hosts —
will lop the branches in one terrifying swing.
Every towering tree will be hacked down.
Every high thing brought to the ground.
10.34:
The dense thickets of Assyria's pride —
felled with iron.
And Lebanon itself will go down before the Mighty One.

11.1:
From Jesse's felled stump a green shoot will push up;
from his roots a Branch will grow and bear fruit.
11.2:
The Spirit of Yahweh will settle and rest on him:
the Spirit of wisdom and understanding,
the Spirit of counsel and might,
the Spirit of knowledge and the reverent fear of Yahweh.
11.3:
His greatest delight will be the fear of Yahweh.
He will not pronounce judgment based on outward appearances,
nor settle a case merely by what he is told,
11.4:
but he will judge for the poor with full righteousness,
and for the earth's humble ones with equity that cannot be bought.
He will strike the earth with nothing but a spoken word,
and with a single breath from his lips he will put the wicked to death.
11.5:
Righteousness buckled at his waist,
faithfulness wrapped close around his hips —
these are what he wears.
11.9:
No harm, no destruction on all my holy mountain —
for the whole earth will be saturated with the knowledge of Yahweh
the way water saturates every last inch of the sea.
11.11:
In that day the Lord will reach out his hand a second time
to reclaim the scattered remnant of his people —
gathering them from Assyria and Egypt,
from Pathros, Cush, and Elam,
from Shinar, Hamath, and the far coastlands of the sea.
11.12:
He will hoist his banner before all the nations
and gather every scattered Israelite;
he will assemble Judah's diaspora
from the four corners of the earth.
11.13:
The ancient rivalry dissolves:
Ephraim's envy of Judah gone,
Judah's hostility toward Ephraim finished.
Neither will taunt the other — the old wound healed at last.
11.14:
United at last, they will sweep down on Philistia to the west,
plunder the eastern peoples together;
Edom and Moab will fall under their hand,
the Ammonites bowing in submission.
11.15:
Yahweh will split the tongue of Egypt's sea
and fan the Euphrates with his scorching breath —
breaking it into seven crossable streams
that people will walk through in sandals.
11.16:
And there will be a great road from Assyria for his remaining people —
the same kind of road that Israel walked
when they came up out of Egypt.

12.1:
In that day you will say:
'Yahweh, I give you praise!
You were angry with me — truly angry —
but your anger has turned,
and you have become my comfort.'
12.2:
'Look — God is my salvation!
I will trust and not be afraid —
for Yah, Yahweh himself, is my strength and my song,
and he is the one who has saved me.'
(The words deliberately echo Moses's song at the sea: Exodus 15:2. The new exodus of chapters 11–12 culminates in the same confession.)
12.3:
Come — draw water from the wells of salvation
with great joy in every bucket.
12.4:
In that day you will call out:
'Praise Yahweh! Invoke his name!
Broadcast his deeds among all the nations —
declare that his name towers above everything!'
12.5:
Sing to Yahweh — he has done magnificent things!
Make it ring in every corner of the earth.
12.6:
Shout! Break into singing, you who live in Zion —
for the Holy One of Israel stands great in your midst!

19.1:
Oracle against Egypt.
Yahweh rides in on a swift cloud, coming into Egypt —
the idols of Egypt shudder at his approach,
and Egypt's courage dissolves within her.
19.2: I will pit Egyptian against Egyptian — brother against brother, neighbor against neighbor, city against city, faction against faction.
19.5:
The Nile's waters will fail;
the river parched and drained dry.
19.7:
The papyrus along the Nile, at every mouth and outflow,
and all the cropland planted along its banks —
will wither, scatter, and disappear.
19.8:
The fishermen will mourn —
those who cast their lines into the Nile will lament,
and those who spread their nets on the water will languish.
19.9:
The workers of combed flax,
the weavers of fine white linen —
they will be put to shame.
19.10:
Egypt's very foundations will crumble;
every wage-worker will be sick with grief.
19.11:
Zoan's princes are fools —
Pharaoh's wisest advisors give nothing but stupid counsel.
How dare they boast to Pharaoh: 'I am a son of the sages, a descendant of ancient kings'?
19.12:
Where are they — your wise men?
Let them tell you! Let them declare
what Yahweh of hosts has determined against Egypt.
19.14:
Yahweh has poured into Egypt a spirit of confusion —
they stagger in everything they undertake,
like a drunkard stumbling through his own vomit.
19.15:
Egypt will find nothing to do —
head or tail, towering palm or lowly reed —
not one of them will accomplish anything.
19.16:
On that day Egypt will be like frightened women,
shaking with terror at the hand Yahweh of hosts raises against her.
19.17:
The very mention of Judah will terrify Egypt —
anyone who speaks the name will fill Egyptian hearts with dread,
because of what Yahweh of hosts has determined against her.
19.18: On that day five Egyptian cities will speak the language of Canaan and swear allegiance to Yahweh of hosts. One will be called the City of the Sun.
19.19:
On that day there will be an altar to Yahweh
in the very heart of Egypt,
and a memorial pillar to Yahweh at its border.
19.20:
It will stand as a sign and witness to Yahweh of hosts in the land of Egypt —
and when they cry to Yahweh because of their oppressors,
he will send them a rescuer and champion who will deliver them.
19.21:
Yahweh will reveal himself to Egypt,
and Egypt will acknowledge Yahweh on that day —
they will worship with sacrifice and grain offering,
make vows to Yahweh and keep them.
19.23:
On that day a highway will run from Egypt all the way to Assyria —
Assyrians coming into Egypt, Egyptians going to Assyria,
and both peoples worshipping Yahweh together.
19.24:
On that day Israel will take its place alongside Egypt and Assyria —
a triad of blessing at the center of the earth —

20.1: The year Sargon king of Assyria sent his commander-in-chief against Ashdod — the year Ashdod fell —
20.2:
at that time Yahweh spoke through Isaiah son of Amoz:
'Go — strip off your sackcloth, take your sandal from your foot.'
He obeyed, going about naked and barefoot.
20.3: Then Yahweh said: 'Just as my servant Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign and omen against Egypt and Cush —'
20.4:
so the king of Assyria will march Egypt and Cush into exile —
young and old together, naked, barefoot, buttocks exposed —
Egypt's shame laid bare for every nation to see.
20.5:
Those who pinned their hope on Cush
and took pride in Egypt
will be terrified and put to shame.
20.6:
On that day the people of this coastland will say:
'Look what has happened to those we counted on —
we ran to them to escape the king of Assyria!
Now how will we get away?'

21.1:
Oracle of the Desert of the Sea.
Like cyclones raging across the Negeb,
it sweeps in — out of the desert, out of a terrible land.
21.2:
A grim vision has been laid before me:
the traitor betrays, the plunderer plunders.
Charge, O Elam! Besiege, O Media!
All the groaning Babylon caused — I have brought it to its end.
21.3:
Therefore my whole body writhes in anguish —
cramps like a woman in labor have seized me.
I am too overcome to hear, too shattered to see.
21.4:
My mind reels; terror has seized me —
the evening I longed for
has been turned into dread.
21.5:
Set the table! Spread the feast!
Eat and drink!
Princes, get up — oil the shields for battle!
21.6: The Lord said to me: 'Go — station a watchman; let him report whatever he sees.'
21.7:
The watchman spotted a column of riders — cavalry in pairs, a train of donkeys, a train of camels —
and strained to hear with every ounce of attention.
21.8:
Then the lookout cried out like a lion:
'My lord! I have stood at the watchtower all day long
and kept my post through every night —'
21.9:
'Look — here comes a column of men, horsemen riding in pairs!'
Then came the answer:
'Fallen! Fallen is Babylon!
Every image of her gods
lies shattered on the ground!'
21.10:
O Israel — my threshed and winnowed people —
what I have heard from Yahweh of hosts, the God of Israel,
I now declare to you.
21.11:
Oracle of Dumah.
A voice calls to me out of Seir:
'Watchman, how much of the night is gone?
Watchman, how much of the night is gone?'
21.12:
The watchman answers:
'Morning is coming — but night comes after it.
If you want to ask, then ask —
but come back again.'
21.13:
Oracle concerning Arabia.
O caravans of the Dedanites,
seek shelter in the thickets of Arabia.
21.14:
O people of Tema, bring water to the fugitives;
go out to meet those who are fleeing — bring them bread.
21.15:
They are fleeing the sword — the drawn blade,
the bent bow, the full fury of war.
21.16:
The Lord told me: 'Within one year —
measured precisely, the way a hired laborer counts his contract —
all of Kedar's glory will be gone.'
21.17:
The archers of Kedar — Kedar's warriors —
will dwindle to almost nothing.
Yahweh, the God of Israel, has declared it.

13.1: The oracle against Babylon — what Isaiah son of Amoz saw.
13.2:
Up — plant the battle standard on the windswept peak!
Raise your voice to them, wave them forward —
let them pour through the gates of the princes!
13.4:
Hear the thunder of it — multitudes on the mountains like a vast army,
the uproar of kingdoms and nations massing together.
Yahweh of hosts is mustering his army for war.
13.5:
They come from the far horizon, from the end of the sky —
Yahweh himself, with every weapon his wrath requires —
to reduce the whole earth to rubble.
13.6:
Wail — the day of Yahweh is almost here!
It comes as destruction straight from the Almighty —
Shaddai himself, the name echoed in the ruin he sends.
13.7:
At that — every hand goes slack,
every human heart dissolves.
13.9:
Here it comes — the day of Yahweh:
cruel, full of wrath, blazing with fierce anger.
The land will be made desolate;
every sinner will be swept from its face.
13.11:
I will visit the world for its evil
and the wicked for the full measure of their guilt.
The pride of the arrogant — I will end it.
The swagger of the violent — I will bring it to the ground.
13.13:
I will shake the heavens;
the earth will lurch from its place —
this is what the wrath of Yahweh of hosts looks like
on the day his anger burns to its height.
13.14:
They will bolt like a hunted gazelle,
scatter like sheep with no one to herd them —
every man rushing back to his own people,
every one fleeing to his own land.
13.15:
Every one they catch — stabbed through.
Every one they take — cut down by the sword.
13.17:
See how I am stirring up the Medes against them —
a people whose ambition runs deeper than silver,
who feel no pull toward gold.
13.19:
Babylon — crown of kingdoms, jewel of Chaldean splendour —
will become like Sodom and Gomorrah
the day God turned them upside down.
13.20:
Never again will it be inhabited —
not for a single generation will anyone settle there.
No desert nomad will pitch a tent on that ground;
no shepherd will bed down his flock within its ruins.

14.1:
For Yahweh will have compassion on Jacob;
he will choose Israel again and settle them in their own land.
Strangers will attach themselves to them,
cleaving to the house of Jacob.
14.2:
Nations will bring them back and set them in their own place;
the house of Israel will inherit them as servants in Yahweh's land —
they who were once slaves will master their former masters,
ruling over those who drove them.
14.3:
When that day comes — when Yahweh gives you rest at last
from the grief and terror and grinding forced labour —
14.4:
— then you will lift this taunt against the king of Babylon and sing:
'How the oppressor has met his end!
The golden city's fury — all of it, gone!'
14.5:
Yahweh has snapped the rod of the wicked,
the sceptre that bruised the nations —
14.6:
the one that thrashed peoples in its rage without ceasing,
that ground nations under its heel with no mercy and no end.
14.7:
The whole earth exhales —
still at last, at rest —
and breaks out singing.
14.8:
Even the cypress trees exult over you,
and the great cedars of Lebanon cry out:
'Since the day you were cut down,
not one woodcutter has come up against us!'
14.9:
Sheol below seethes with excitement at your arrival —
rousing the shades of the dead to greet you,
all who were once kings and chiefs of the earth,
lifted from their thrones for the occasion.
14.10:
And every one of them speaks:
'So — you have joined us at last!
You who were so great — you have become like us.
Weak. Just like us.'
14.11:
All that pomp — dragged down to Sheol.
The noise of your harps — silenced in the pit.
Maggots are your mattress;
worms are your blanket.
14.12:
How you have plummeted from heaven —
O Day Star, brilliant son of the dawn!
Hurled to earth, flat on the ground —
you who once forced nations to their knees!
14.14:
'I will climb above the topmost clouds;
I will be like El Elyon — the Most High himself.'
14.15:
Instead — you are thrown down to Sheol,
down to the lowest reaches of the pit.
14.16:
Those who see you will stare long and hard,
then say with disbelief:
'Is this the man — this wretched thing —
who made the whole earth tremble,
who shook the thrones of kingdoms?'
14.17:
'The one who turned the world into a wasteland,
who smashed city after city to rubble,
who never opened the prison door for a single captive?'
14.18:
Every other king lies in dignity —
each one buried with honour in his own tomb.
14.19:
But you — thrown out, away from your tomb,
like a revolting twig nobody wants,
wrapped in the bodies of those run through by the sword,
spilling down to the stony pit like a carcass crushed underfoot.
14.20:
No grave for you beside the other kings —
you who devastated your own land,
you who slaughtered your own people.
The line of evildoers will be blotted from every record.
14.21:
Make ready the execution ground for his sons —
the guilt of the fathers falls on them —
lest they rise and reclaim the earth,
covering the whole world's face with rebuilt cities.
14.22:
'I myself will rise against them,' declares Yahweh of hosts.
'I will erase from Babylon every name, every remnant,
every child and grandchild,' declares Yahweh.
14.23:
'I will hand it over to the hedgehog and fill it with standing pools —
then sweep what remains away with the broom of destruction,' declares Yahweh of hosts.
14.24:
Yahweh of hosts has sworn with an oath:
'Whatever I have planned — it will happen.
Whatever I have purposed — it will stand.'
14.25:
'I will break Assyria in my own land —
trample him on my own mountains.
Then his yoke will be lifted from my people's necks
and his crushing load from their shoulders.'
14.26:
This is the plan laid out for the whole earth;
this is the hand stretched out over every nation.
14.27:
Yahweh of hosts has decided — who will cancel it?
His hand is outstretched — who will force it back?
14.28: In the year of King Ahaz's death, this word arrived:
14.29:
Do not celebrate, O Philistia — every part of you —
because the rod that struck you has been broken.
For from that serpent's root another serpent will rise,
and its offspring — a winged, venomous terror.
14.30:
The most destitute of the poor will graze in safety;
the needy will lie down without fear.
But your root — I will kill it with famine,
and every last survivor will be cut down.
14.31:
Wail at the gates! Cry out from the walls!
Philistia — every part of you — melt in terror!
For from the north a column of smoke rises —
and in that advancing army, not a single straggler.
14.32:
What will you say when the envoys of the nations demand an answer?
This: Yahweh has laid Zion's foundations —
and in her the weakest of his people have their sure refuge.

15.1:
The oracle against Moab:
Because Ar of Moab is laid waste in a single night — Moab is undone.
Because Kir of Moab is laid waste in a single night — Moab is undone.
15.2:
They go up to the temple and to Dibon,
climbing to the high places to weep.
Over Nebo and over Medeba — wailing.
Every head shaved bald, every beard cut in mourning.
15.5:
My heart aches for Moab —
his refugees fleeing to Zoar, to Eglath-shelishiyah.
Up the slope of Luhith they trudge, weeping at every step;
on the Horonaim road the shout of ruin rises up.
15.6:
The waters of Nimrim have gone dry —
the grass scorched, the young shoots dead,
not a trace of green remaining.
15.7:
Whatever they have stored and laid by —
they carry it all across the Brook of the Willows into exile.
15.8:
The wailing has encircled every border of Moab —
it rings out as far as Eglaim,
it carries all the way to Beer-elim.
15.9:
The waters of Dimon are already full of blood —
but I will bring more upon Dimon yet:
a lion waiting for those who flee from Moab,
a lion for every survivor left in the land.

16.2:
Like a bird flushed from its nest —
like young ones scattered with nowhere to land —
that is what Moab's daughters are like at the fords of the Arnon.
16.5:
Then a throne will be established in hesed — in covenant faithfulness —
and on it, in the tent of David, one will sit in truth:
judging, pursuing justice,
swift to do what is right.
16.6:
Everyone has heard of Moab's pride — how utterly puffed up he is,
his arrogance, his insolence, his seething fury.
All his boasting is hollow air.
16.8:
The terraces of Heshbon languish;
the vine of Sibmah — whose branches once arched
all the way to Jazer and out across the desert,
reaching even to the sea —
its rulers have been struck down, its vines laid waste.
16.9:
So I weep for Sibmah's vine with Jazer's own tears;
I drench you with my grief, O Heshbon, O Elealeh —
for the battle shout has crashed down
upon your summer fruit and your gathered harvest.
16.10:
Joy and gladness are stripped from the fertile fields.
In the vineyards — no song, no harvest shout.
No one treads the winepress;
I have silenced the shout of the vintage.
16.11:
My heart vibrates like a harp in grief for Moab;
my innermost being mourns for Kir-hareseth.
16.12:
When Moab drags himself to the high place,
tiring himself out with prayer at the sanctuary —
he will get nothing. He cannot prevail.
16.13: This was the word Yahweh spoke concerning Moab long before.
16.14:
But now Yahweh speaks with new precision:
'In three years — counted as precisely as a hired worker counts his contract —
Moab's glory will be despised, for all its great multitude.
The remnant will be pathetically small, weak, and of no account.'

17.1:
The oracle against Damascus:
Damascus will be stripped of its city-status —
reduced to a heap of rubble.
17.2:
The towns of Aroer will stand empty —
wild flocks will bed down there
and nothing will frighten them away.
17.3:
The fortresses of Ephraim will be gone;
Damascus will lose its sovereignty —
and what is left of Syria will be as diminished
as the fading glory of Israel herself,
declares Yahweh of hosts.
17.4:
In that day Jacob's glory will dim
and the richness of his flesh will waste to bone.
17.5:
Think of a reaper sweeping the standing grain,
his arm gathering the ears —
like a gleaner working the last rows
after the harvest in the Valley of Rephaim.
17.6:
A few gleanings will remain —
like the last olives after the beating:
two or three at the tip of the topmost branch,
four or five on the outer limbs —
declares Yahweh, the God of Israel.
17.9:
In that day their fortress cities will be like the abandoned high places in the forest —
deserted as those hilltops the Israelites once left behind —
nothing but desolation.
17.10:
Because you have forgotten the God of your salvation —
you have not remembered the Rock, your fortress —
so now you plant decorative gardens
and set in cuttings from pagan vines,
17.11:
You can make them shoot up the morning after planting;
you can force them into blossom by dawn —
but on the day of grief, on the day of pain that cannot be healed,
the harvest will vanish.
17.12:
Woe to the thunder of many peoples —
they roar like the crashing of the seas!
Woe to the surge of nations —
they rush and boom like the torrents of mighty waters!
17.13:
They surge — but he rebukes them,
and they flee to the far distance:
chased like chaff blown off a mountain in the wind,
like a column of dust that a storm whips away to nothing.
17.14:
Evening falls — terror.
Before dawn — nothing remains.
This is the share of those who pillage us;
this is the lot of those who plunder us.

18.1:
Woe to the land of whirring wings —
the land that lies beyond the rivers of Cush!
18.2:
This land sends its messengers by sea —
papyrus boats skimming the waterways —
'Go, swift ones, to the tall and smooth-skinned people,
the nation feared everywhere near and far,
mighty and aggressive, whose land the great rivers divide.'
18.3:
All inhabitants of the world, all who dwell upon the earth —
when the standard is raised on the mountains: look!
When the trumpet sounds: listen!
18.4:
For Yahweh told me this:
'I will be still in my dwelling place — watching, without a word —
like the clear heat that glimmers in the sunshine,
like a cloud of dew forming in the heat of the harvest.'
18.5:
Then — before the harvest,
when the flower has given way to a ripening grape —
he will take the pruning hook to the shoots;
he will lop off and clear away every spreading branch.
18.6:
All of it left behind —
for the mountain birds of prey and for the beasts of the field.
The vultures will summer on the carrion;
the wild beasts will winter on it.
18.7:
In that time, a gift will be brought to Yahweh of hosts:
from the tall and smooth-skinned people,
from the nation feared near and far —
mighty and dominant, their land cut by great rivers —
brought to Mount Zion,
the place where the name of Yahweh of hosts dwells.

22.1:
An oracle: the Valley of Vision.
What has gotten into you? Why is everyone up on the rooftops?
22.2:
You roar with noise and clamour — a city drunk on its own celebration!
But your dead were not killed in combat.
No sword brought them down, no battle claimed them.
22.3:
Every last commander fled — no battle was even fought.
They surrendered without an arrow loosed.
Everyone they found was taken prisoner —
even those who had run to the far horizon.
22.4:
So I say: leave me alone. Let me weep without restraint.
Do not rush to console me.
The daughter of my people has been destroyed —
I cannot stop mourning.
22.5:
For the Lord Yahweh of hosts has appointed a day —
a day of trampling and terror and confusion —
here in the Valley of Vision.
Walls collapsing, battle-cries ricocheting off the mountains.
22.6:
Elam manned the quivers, with chariotry and cavalry in formation;
Kir stripped the shield covers — ready for war.
22.7:
Your best valleys choked with chariots;
cavalry stationed at every gate.
22.8:
He stripped away every shield Judah had.
And what did Jerusalem do? You ran to the weapons stockpiled in the House of the Forest.
22.9:
You counted the breaches in David's city wall — too many to ignore —
and channelled the lower pool's water inward for the siege.
22.10:
You catalogued Jerusalem's houses
and tore some of them down to reinforce the city wall.
22.11:
You dug a cistern between the walls to collect water from the old pool —
every calculation made, every plan executed.
But you never once looked to the One who made all of it,
never gave a thought to the One who had designed this whole scene long before you were born.
22.12:
The Lord Yahweh of hosts was calling for grief that day —
mourning, weeping, shaved heads, sackcloth:
22.13:
Instead — a party.
Oxen butchered, sheep slaughtered, wine poured out, feasting at full swing.
'Eat and drink! Tomorrow we die!' That was the cry.
22.14:
Then the word came directly into my ears — from Yahweh of hosts:
'I swear it: this guilt will not be wiped away.
You will carry it to your grave.'
The Lord Yahweh of hosts declares it.
22.15:
Then the Lord Yahweh of hosts sent this word:
'Go to this steward — Shebna, the one who manages the palace —'
22.16:
'What gives you any right to be here?
Who do you think you are, cutting yourself a grand tomb up on the heights —
carving your burial chamber out of the living rock
as though this were your permanent home?'
22.17:
'Look out — Yahweh is about to hurl you away with a mighty overhand throw, you man!
He will lay hold of you'
22.18:
'— ball you up and hurl you like a stone across a wide, open land.
There you will die. Your prized chariots left useless there.
You — the disgrace of your lord's house!'
22.19:
'I am removing you from your post.
Down from your position you will come.'
22.20: 'And in that day I will call my servant Eliakim, Hilkiah's son,'
22.21:
'I will dress him in your official robes and cinch your sash around him.
Your authority I will place in his hands.
He will be a father — a true, caring father — to Jerusalem's people and to Judah's household.'
22.22:
'I will lay the key of David's house on his shoulder.
When he opens, no one shuts;
when he shuts, no one opens.'
(Revelation 3:7 applies this verse directly to Christ, the ultimate heir of David's throne and the one who holds the keys of death and Hades.)
22.23:
'I will hammer him in like a peg driven into a solid wall —
a fixed point of honour and glory for his entire family.'
22.24:
'And then every dependant of his father's house will load themselves onto him —
offspring and hangers-on, from the smallest cup to the largest jar,
every last piece of the family's glory hung on this one peg.'
22.25:
'But in that day,' Yahweh of hosts declares,
'even that firmly-set peg will give way.
It will be cut down and collapse.
And every weight that was hanging on it will crash to the ground.'
The LORD has spoken it.

23.1:
An oracle: Tyre.
Howl, ships of Tarshish! Wail!
For Tyre is destroyed — harbour and house alike gone.
The news reaches you from across the sea, from Cyprus.
23.2:
Fall silent, you coastland people.
Sidon's traders who crossed the great sea — they were the ones who filled your coffers.
23.3:
Egypt's grain shipped down the Nile, harvested from the River's broad banks —
that was the cargo that made Tyre rich.
She was the marketplace where all the nations came to trade.
23.4:
Sidon, be ashamed!
For the sea itself — the sea, that great stronghold of power — says:
'I never laboured in childbirth.
I have no sons I raised up, no daughters I nurtured.'
(Sidon was Tyre's founding city; Tyre's ruin is the sea's admission of childlessness.)
23.5: When Egypt hears the news of Tyre's fall, they will writhe in anguish.
23.6:
Flee to Tarshish! Sail away!
Howl, you coastland people — howl!
23.7:
Is this really it — the city that once rang with joy?
The city whose roots reach back to ancient days?
The city whose people sailed the world to plant colonies across the sea?
This is Tyre?
23.8:
Who could have planned this against Tyre?
Tyre — the city that wore a crown and handed out titles!
Tyre, whose merchants were princes,
whose traders were the most celebrated names on earth?
23.9:
Yahweh of hosts planned it.
His purpose: to puncture the pride of every glittering glory,
to humiliate every great name the earth has ever honoured.
23.10:
Daughter of Tarshish, flow through your own land like the Nile now —
there is nothing left to restrain you.
The docking fees, the harbour tolls, the Tyrian merchants — all gone.
23.11:
Yahweh stretched his hand across the sea
and shook the kingdoms at their roots.
He issued the order against Canaan:
Demolish every fortress.
23.12:
'Your days of exulting are over,
O bruised and ruined daughter of Sidon.
Get up — sail to Cyprus.
But even there, you will not rest.'
23.13:
Look at the Chaldeans!
That people — barely a power until Assyria built them up —
they are the ones who raised siege towers,
tore down every grand building,
and reduced Tyre to rubble.
23.14:
Ships of Tarshish — howl again!
Your safe haven is gone. Your source of wealth — laid waste.
23.15:
In that day Tyre will drop from memory for seventy years —
the length of one king's reign.
At the end of those seventy years she will come back,
but her return will be like the song of a forgotten prostitute:
23.16:
'Take your harp and walk the streets —
you who have been forgotten, you old prostitute!
Play sweetly, sing every song you know,
so someone, somewhere, will still remember you.'
23.17:
At the end of seventy years, Yahweh will deal with Tyre again.
She will go back to business — back to selling herself
to every kingdom across the face of the earth.
23.18:
But this time her profits will be consecrated to Yahweh —
not hoarded, not stored away.
Her merchandise will feed and clothe those who dwell before Yahweh,
given freely, held lightly, made holy.

24.1:
Look — Yahweh is emptying the earth.
Wasting it. Warping its surface. Scattering every living soul.
24.2:
No one is exempt:
priest and people — the same fate.
Servant and master, maid and mistress,
buyer and seller, lender and borrower,
creditor and debtor:
all of them equally undone.
24.3:
Utterly emptied. Utterly plundered.
Yahweh has decreed it, and that settles it.
24.4:
The earth grieves; it withers.
The world droops and fades.
Even the proudest people on earth collapse.
24.5:
The earth is defiled beneath the weight of its people —
because they violated the laws of creation,
ignored every ordinance,
and shattered the everlasting covenant that bound heaven to earth.
24.6:
So the curse is eating the earth alive.
Its people bear the full weight of their guilt.
The population is burned away —
only a scattered handful left standing.
24.7:
The new wine is gone; the vine shrivels.
Every glad heart has turned to groaning.
24.8:
The tambourines fall silent.
The noise of the celebrating crowd — finished.
All the music of joy — gone.
24.9:
No more singing over the wine.
Every cup of it turns bitter in the mouth.
24.10:
The city of chaos is smashed — undone, returned to the formless void it came from.
Every door shut, every house sealed tight,
no one goes in.
24.11:
In the streets people are crying out for wine.
All joy has gone dark.
Every trace of gladness banished from the land.
24.12:
Inside the city: desolation.
At the city gate: rubble, shattered beyond repair.
24.13:
This is what will remain when it is all over,
across the whole earth and all its peoples:
what you find when you've beaten an olive tree for every last fruit,
what gleaners find when the vintage is through.
24.14:
Then — voices rising from the west:
they are singing!
Shouting for joy!
The greatness of Yahweh ringing out from across the sea.
24.15:
From the east, glorify Yahweh!
From the islands and coastlands of the sea,
lift high the name of Yahweh — the God of Israel!
24.16:
From the far ends of the earth I hear songs rising — praise songs for the Righteous One.
But I cry out: 'I am wasting away — wasting away! Woe to me!
The treacherous ones are still at it;
the traitors going on and on in their treachery!'
24.17:
Terror and the pit and the trap —
these are coming for everyone who lives on the earth!
24.18:
Run from the terror — and fall into the pit.
Climb out of the pit — and get caught in the trap.
There is no escape route.
The windows of heaven have been thrown wide open;
the foundations of the earth are shaking.
24.19:
The earth is cracking apart.
The earth is splitting open.
The earth is convulsing —
24.20:
— staggering like a drunk who cannot stand.
Swaying like a flimsy field-hut in a storm.
Its own accumulated sin is too heavy to bear.
Down it goes — and will not rise again.
24.21:
In that day Yahweh will call to account
the powers that hold dominion in the sky above
and the kings who hold dominion on the earth below.
24.22:
They will be rounded up like prisoners,
herded into a pit, locked in a dungeon.
And after a long, long time —
judgment will finally come.
24.23:
Then the moon itself will be put to shame;
the sun will blush at its own dimness —
because Yahweh of hosts reigns.
Reigns on Mount Zion.
Reigns in Jerusalem.
His glory blazing before the council of his elders.

29.1:
Woe to Ariel! To Ariel —
city where David pitched his camp!
Add year to year; let the sacred feasts keep coming round —
29.2:
yet I will lay siege to Ariel,
and there will be mourning and anguish —
and she will be to me what her name says: an altar hearth, a fire pit, consumed.
29.3:
I will ring you round with a siege camp,
batter you with earthworks,
and raise fortifications against you.
29.4:
Then you will be laid flat —
you will speak from the ground,
your words barely a whisper rising from the dust,
your voice like a ghost moaning up from the earth,
your speech a murmur scraped from the dust.
29.5:
But the horde of your enemies will crumble to fine dust,
the multitude of tyrants scatter like windblown chaff —
and then, in an instant, suddenly —
29.6:
Yahweh of hosts will appear:
thunder, earthquake, shattering noise —
whirlwind, tempest, the flame of consuming fire.
29.7:
All the nations that mass against Ariel —
all who besiege her, press her hard, assault her walls —
will dissolve like a dream, a vision of the night.
29.8:
Like a hungry man who dreams of a feast
and wakes with his stomach still empty,
or a thirsty man who dreams of a long drink
and wakes faint, still craving —
so will every nation that masses against Mount Zion wake up.
29.9:
Stand stupefied — stay there bewildered.
Be blind — go on being blind.
They are drunk, but not with wine;
they stagger, but not with strong drink.
29.10:
For Yahweh has poured over you a spirit of stupor —
he has shut your eyes: those who call themselves prophets.
He has veiled your heads: those who call themselves seers.
29.11:
All of this vision has become for you
like a sealed book —
hand it to someone who can read and say, 'Read this,' and he says,
'I can't — it's sealed.'
29.12:
Hand it to someone who can't read and say, 'Read this,' and he answers,
'I don't know how to read.'
Neither one can open it.
29.13:
And the Lord declared:
'Because this people approaches me with their mouth
and honors me with their lips,
while their heart is nowhere near me —
and their awe of me is nothing but a rule
drilled into them by other humans —'
29.14:
'therefore watch — I am about to do something among this people
that will leave everyone astonished:
the wisdom of the wise will collapse,
and the insight of the shrewd will disappear.'
29.15:
Woe to those who burrow deep to hide their schemes from Yahweh,
who do their work in the dark and say,
'Who can see us? Who knows what we're doing?'
29.16:
What a reversal you have made!
Shall the clay treat the potter as its equal?
Shall the pot say to the one who made it, 'You didn't make me'?
Shall the vessel say to its maker, 'You don't understand anything'?
29.17:
Only a little while longer —
and Lebanon will be turned into an orchard,
and what is now orchard will seem like ancient forest.
29.18:
On that day the deaf will hear
the words of a scroll —
and out of deep gloom and darkness
the eyes of the blind will see.
29.19:
The meek will find fresh joy in Yahweh,
and the poorest of the poor will exult in the Holy One of Israel.
29.20:
For the tyrant will be finished,
the mocker will be gone,
and everyone who lies in wait to do evil will be cut off —
29.21:
those who trap a man with a single word,
who set snares for the one who dares to speak truth at the city gate,
who twist nothing into a charge and deny the innocent their rights.
29.22:
Therefore this is what Yahweh says —
the one who redeemed Abraham —
concerning the house of Jacob:
'Jacob will no longer be put to shame;
his face will no longer turn pale with disgrace.'
29.23:
When he sees his children in his midst —
the work of my own hands —
they will sanctify my name;
they will treat the Holy One of Jacob as holy
and stand in reverent awe before the God of Israel.
29.24:
Those who had gone spiritually astray will come to their senses,
and those who had nothing but complaints
will finally receive instruction.

30.1:
Woe, rebellious children — Yahweh's word —
who make plans but not mine,
who forge alliances but not by my Spirit,
stacking sin upon sin —
30.2:
who set out to go down to Egypt
without a word to me —
who want to shore up their lives in Pharaoh's strength
and take shelter in Egypt's shadow!
30.3:
So Pharaoh's power will become your disgrace,
and Egypt's shadow the very thing that humiliates you.
30.4:
Even with his envoys positioned at Zoan
and his ambassadors traveling all the way to Hanes —
30.5:
everyone will come away humiliated
by a nation that cannot deliver —
no help, no advantage, nothing but shame and reproach.
30.6:
Oracle of the beasts of the Negeb.
Through a land of trouble and anguish,
of roaring lion and prowling lioness,
of viper and winged serpent —
they load their wealth onto young donkeys
and their treasures onto camels' backs,
and trek it to a people that cannot help them.
30.7:
Egypt's help is nothing — an empty illusion.
So I have named her: Rahab Who Sits Still —
the great sea-dragon that does not move.
30.9:
For this is a rebellious people,
children of deception —
children who will not hear Yahweh's instruction,
30.10:
who say to the seers, 'Stop seeing,'
and to the prophets, 'Don't give us the truth —
tell us what we want to hear; prophesy illusions;'
30.11:
'get out of the road, clear off the path —
we don't want to hear another word
about the Holy One of Israel.'
30.12:
Therefore this is what the Holy One of Israel says:
'Because you despise this word
and put your confidence in oppression and deviousness and lean on them —'
30.13:
'this sin will hang over you
like a crack in a high wall,
bulging outward,
ready to give way —
and when it falls, it falls in an instant.'
30.14:
'The collapse will be like a clay pot
smashed without mercy —
so completely shattered
that no piece remains large enough
to carry an ember from the fire
or dip a sip of water from a tank.'
30.15:
For this is what the Lord Yahweh, the Holy One of Israel, declared:
'In returning and rest — there is your salvation.
In quietness and trust — there is your strength.'
But you refused.
30.16:
You said, 'No — we'll outrun them on horses.'
So you will flee.
'We'll ride swift horses.'
So your pursuers will be swifter.
30.17:
A thousand will scatter at the shout of one;
at the shout of five, you will all run —
until you are left as solitary as a pole on a mountain peak,
a signal flag on a bare hill.
30.18:
Therefore Yahweh waits — waits to be gracious to you;
he rises up to show you mercy.
For Yahweh is a God of justice.
Blessed are all who wait for him.
30.19:
O people who dwell in Zion, in Jerusalem —
you will weep no more.
He will be truly gracious to you
the moment your cry rises —
the moment he hears it, he answers.
30.20:
Even if the Lord makes you eat the bread of hardship
and drink the water of affliction —
your Teacher will not hide himself any longer:
your own eyes will see your Teacher.
30.21:
And your ears will catch a voice behind you:
'This is the way — walk in it' —
whenever you drift to the right or to the left.
30.22:
Then you will treat your silver-plated idols as filth
and your gold-overlaid images as unclean —
flung away like something unclean,
your word to them: 'Away with you. Gone.'
30.23:
He will send rain for the seed you plant —
the land will give back rich, abundant bread.
On that day your cattle will graze
in broad, open pastures.
30.24:
Even the oxen and donkeys that work the fields
will eat well-cured, seasoned fodder —
winnowed clean with shovel and fork.
30.25:
On every high mountain, on every lofty hill,
there will be streams and channels of water —
on the day of the great slaughter,
when the towers come down.
30.26:
And the light of the moon will be
as bright as the sun once was,
and the light of the sun seven times over —
seven days' worth of sunlight blazing at once —
on the day Yahweh binds up the wounds of his people
and heals the blow he himself delivered.
30.27:
The name of Yahweh is coming — coming from far off,
burning with fury, thick with billowing smoke;
his lips full of wrath,
his tongue a tongue of devouring fire —
30.28:
his breath an overflowing torrent
that rises up to the neck,
sifting nations through a sieve of ruin,
driving a bit into the jaws of every people
that leads them to their end.
30.29:
But for you there will be a song —
like the song that rises in the night of a sacred festival,
deep joy of heart,
like walking to the sound of a flute
up to the mountain of Yahweh, to the Rock of Israel.
30.30:
Yahweh will thunder in his majestic voice
and reveal the sweep of his descending arm —
fuming with rage,
a flame of consuming fire,
cloudburst, tempest, hailstones crashing down.
30.31:
At the voice of Yahweh the Assyrian shatters —
struck down by the rod he swings.
30.32:
Every blow of Yahweh's punishing rod
will be played to tambourine and lyre —
and with arm swinging high he will fight against them.
30.33:
For Topheth — the burning place — has been ready since ancient times;
yes, it has been prepared for the king.
Deep and wide is its pyre,
fire and wood in abundance —
and the breath of Yahweh,
like a torrent of brimstone,
sets it ablaze.

25.1:
O Yahweh, you are my God —
I lift you high, I praise your name,
for you have done wonders:
your ancient purposes fulfilled, every one of them faithful and sure.
25.2:
You turned a great city into rubble,
a fortress into a ruin;
the stronghold of aliens is a city no more —
never to be rebuilt.
25.3:
So strong peoples will give you glory;
the city of ruthless nations will tremble before you.
25.4:
You have been a fortress for the helpless,
a shelter for the needy in their trouble —
a refuge from the tempest,
shade against the scorching heat.
For the ruthless come like a wall-shattering storm.
25.5:
You silence the roar of alien nations —
as cloud-shadow kills the heat of the open desert,
so the boasting of the ruthless
you bring down to nothing.
25.6:
On this mountain Yahweh of hosts will spread a banquet for all peoples —
rich food, aged wine,
the finest cuts of meat,
the most carefully strained vintages.
Every nation is invited.
25.7:
On this mountain he will tear away
the shroud of grief that covers every nation,
the death-veil spread over all peoples.
25.8:
He will swallow death — swallow it forever.
The Lord Yahweh will wipe every tear from every face;
the shame his people have borne
he will lift from all the earth.
Yahweh has spoken it.
25.9:
On that day they will say:
'Here he is — this is our God!
We waited for him, and now he has saved us.
This is Yahweh — we waited for him!
Now we rejoice and shout for joy in his deliverance!'
25.10:
The hand of Yahweh will rest on this mountain —
but Moab will be trampled beneath it,
as straw is pressed down into a dung heap.
25.11:
He will press Moab down
as a swimmer pushes the water aside with every stroke —
until their pride collapses
and all their scheming comes to nothing.
25.12:
Your towering walls, your loftiest fortresses —
he will bring them down, lay them flat,
and cast them into the dust.

26.1:
On that day this song will be sung in the land of Judah:
'We have a strong city —
God himself has set salvation as our walls,
as ramparts around us.'
26.2:
Throw open the gates!
Let the righteous nation enter —
the people who keep faith.
26.3:
The mind that rests on you
you hold in perfect peace —
because it trusts in you.
26.6:
And who will walk across it?
The poor — their feet will stamp it down.
The needy — their steps will claim it.
26.7:
The path of the righteous runs level —
you who are perfectly just
smooth the road before them.
26.8:
Walking the path your judgments lay down, O Yahweh —
we have waited for you.
The longing of our whole being is for your name,
for every memory of you.
26.9:
In the night my whole being reaches out for you;
deep within me my spirit longs for you.
For when your judgments fall on the earth,
the people of the world learn what righteousness means.
26.10:
Show mercy to the wicked — still they refuse to learn what is right.
Even in a land of uprightness they go on acting unjustly,
blind to the majesty of Yahweh.
26.11:
Yahweh, your hand is raised —
but they refuse to see it.
Let them finally see your burning zeal for your people,
and let shame fall on them.
Let the fire prepared for your enemies devour them.
26.12:
Yahweh, you will establish peace for us —
for everything we have done, you accomplished it in us.
26.13:
O Yahweh, our God —
other lords have dominated us,
but you alone are the one we name and acknowledge.
26.15:
You have made the nation great, O Yahweh —
great and greater still, to your own glory —
pushing its borders out to every corner of the land.
26.16:
Yahweh, in their distress they finally came to you —
when your discipline pressed down on them,
they poured out their prayers in whispers.
26.17:
Like a woman full-term with child,
who writhes and cries out as the birth approaches —
that is how we have been before you, O Yahweh.
26.18:
We conceived, we labored —
but gave birth to nothing but wind.
We accomplished no deliverance for the earth;
the oppressors of the world still stand.
26.19:
Your dead will live — yes, even my own body will rise!
Awake and sing for joy, you who sleep in the dust —
for your dew is the dew of a new morning,
and the earth will give birth to the long-dead.
26.20:
Come, my people — enter your inner rooms,
shut your doors behind you.
Hide yourself for just a little while
until the fury has swept past.
26.21:
See — Yahweh comes out of his place
to punish the earth's inhabitants for all their sin.
The earth will bring its hidden bloodshed to light;
no longer will it cover its murdered dead.

27.1:
On that day Yahweh will draw his great, fierce sword —
his mighty, well-honed blade —
and strike down Leviathan, the swift darting serpent,
Leviathan, the coiling monster of the deep,
and slay the dragon lurking in the sea.
27.2:
On that day, sing this song:
'A vineyard — bursting with the finest wine!'
27.4:
I have no anger against my vineyard.
If briers and thorns rise against me in battle,
I will march through them and burn them down.
27.5:
Let anyone who would oppose me
take hold of my strength instead —
let them come and make peace with me.
Let them make peace with me.
27.7:
Has God struck his people the way he struck those who oppressed them?
Has Israel been slaughtered like those she slaughtered?
By no means — the discipline was far lighter than the destruction.
27.8:
Measured, measured — that is how he dealt with her:
driving her into exile with the fierce east wind,
the scorching desert blast.
Nothing beyond what was needed.
27.9:
This is how Jacob's sin will be atoned for —
the full result of its removal:
every altar stone smashed to chalk,
no Asherah pole and no incense altar left standing.
When that is done, the guilt is gone.
27.10:
The once-fortified city stands desolate;
every house abandoned, a wilderness now —
calves graze there, lie down there,
stripping every last branch.
27.11:
The branches wither; women come to snap them off for firewood.
This is a people without understanding —
so their Maker will have no compassion on them,
their Creator will show them no mercy.
27.12:
On that day Yahweh will thresh his grain —
from the great River down to the Brook of Egypt —
and you will be gathered up one by one,
O people of Israel.
27.13:
On that day a great trumpet will sound —
the lost ones languishing in Assyria,
the exiles scattered through Egypt —
they will come and worship Yahweh
on the holy mountain in Jerusalem.

28.1:
Woe to Ephraim's drunkards — woe to their garland of pride!
That glorious flower, already fading,
perched on the head of the lush valley —
the crown of those drunk on wine.
28.2:
Watch — the Lord has a mighty agent ready:
like a hailstorm, like a raging tempest,
like a torrent of floodwaters sweeping everything away —
he will dash Ephraim's proud crown to the ground.
28.3:
The proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards
will be ground underfoot.
28.4:
That glorious crown on the head of the fertile valley —
it will fade like a flower,
vanish like an early fig in summer:
someone spots it, snatches it from the branch,
and it is gone in an instant.
28.5:
On that day the LORD of hosts will be the crown —
glorious, beautiful —
for every one of his remnant people.
28.6:
He will be a spirit of justice for those who must judge,
and strength for those who hold the gate against the enemy.
28.7:
But even these — the priest and the prophet — reel with wine,
stagger from strong drink.
Swallowed up by wine, wobbling with drink,
they err in their visions
