and stumble over every ruling they make.
28.8:
Every table in the place is covered with vomit and filth —
not one clean spot to be found.
28.9:
Who does he think he is teaching?
Who can possibly understand his message?
Toddlers just weaned from the breast?
The drunken leaders mock the prophet's repeated warnings as fit only for infants.
28.10:
'Rule on rule, rule on rule —
line on line, line on line —
a little here, a little there!'
They mimic the prophet's warnings as tedious, meaningless babble.
28.11:
Therefore God will speak to this people
through foreign stutterers, through a strange language —
through Assyrian conquerors, since they refused to hear his prophets.
28.12:
He said to his people: 'Come here — rest!
Let the weary find rest here.
This is the refreshment you need!' —
But they refused to listen.
28.13:
So the word of Yahweh becomes to them
rule on rule, line on line —
a little here, a little there —
until they stumble backward,
are broken and snared and taken captive.
28.14:
Hear the word of Yahweh, you scoffers —
you who govern this people in Jerusalem.
28.15:
You have said: 'We have a deal with Death;
we have a pact with Sheol, the realm of the dead.
When the flood of judgment sweeps through,
it won't touch us —
we've built our shelter out of lies,
our hiding place out of deception.'
28.16:
Therefore the Lord Yahweh declares:
'See — I am setting a stone in Zion:
a tested stone, a precious cornerstone,
a sure and solid foundation.
Whoever trusts in it will never panic or flee.'
28.17:
I will use justice as my measuring line,
righteousness as my level —
and the hailstorm will sweep away your refuge of lies,
the floodwaters will overwhelm your hiding place.
28.18:
Your deal with Death will be torn up;
your pact with Sheol will not hold.
When the flood of judgment sweeps through,
you will be crushed beneath it.
28.19:
Every time it sweeps through, it will take you —
morning after morning, day and night.
Just grasping what is happening
will be terror enough.
28.21:
Yahweh will rise as he rose at Perazim,
stir himself as in the valley of Gibeon —
but this time to do his strange work,
his altogether alien act:
to judge his own people, not their enemies.
28.22:
Stop mocking — before your chains grow tighter.
I have heard it from the Lord Yahweh of hosts:
a decree of utter destruction upon the whole earth.
28.23:
Listen — hear my voice;
lean in and hear what I am saying.
28.24:
Does the farmer spend all day plowing just to plant a seed?
Does he break up clods without end?
28.25:
No — when the ground is ready, he plants with precision:
caraway here, cumin scattered there,
wheat in rows, barley in its allotted place,
spelt along the border.
Each seed gets exactly what it needs.
28.26:
His God has taught him;
trained him to know exactly what to do.
28.27:
You don't thresh caraway with a threshing sledge —
or run the cartwheel over cumin.
Caraway gets a stick; cumin gets a rod.
Each crop gets exactly the right treatment.
28.28:
Grain for bread must be crushed —
but no farmer threshes it forever.
He may drive his cartwheel across it,
let horses trample it —
but he never grinds it to dust.
The grain survives to become bread.
28.29:
All this wisdom comes from Yahweh of hosts —
wonderful in counsel,
excellent in everything he does.

31.1:
Woe to those who go down to Egypt for help,
staking everything on horses,
trusting in chariots because there are so many
and in cavalry because it is so strong —
but not looking to the Holy One of Israel,
not seeking Yahweh.
31.2:
But Yahweh too is wise — and he brings disaster.
He does not take back his word.
He will rise against the household of evildoers
and against those who support the wicked.
31.3:
The Egyptians are human — not God;
their horses are flesh — not spirit.
When Yahweh extends his hand,
both helper and helped will stumble and fall —
and all will perish together.
31.4:
For this is what Yahweh said to me:
As a lion, a young lion, crouches growling over its prey
and a whole pack of shepherds is called out against it —
unfrightened by their shouts,
unmoved by all their noise —
so Yahweh of hosts will come down
to wage war on Mount Zion and its hill.
31.5:
Like birds hovering over their nest,
so Yahweh of hosts will cover Jerusalem —
covering, he will deliver it;
passing over, he will rescue it.
31.6:
Return — return to the one
from whom the children of Israel have strayed so far.
31.7:
On that day everyone will throw away
their silver idols and golden images —
things their own hands crafted
that became their downfall.
31.8:
The Assyrian will fall — but not by any human sword.
No mortal blade will cut him down.
He will flee from before the sword,
and his young warriors will be taken, made to serve.
31.9:
His stronghold will crumble in terror;
his commanders will flee in panic at the sight of Yahweh's battle standard —
declares Yahweh,
whose fire burns in Zion,
whose furnace blazes in Jerusalem.

32.1:
Look — a king will reign in righteousness,
and his rulers will govern with justice.
32.2:
Each one of them will be a shelter from the wind,
a refuge from the storm —
like streams of water in the desert,
like the cool shadow of a great rock
in a scorching, exhausted land.
32.3:
Eyes that can see will no longer be shut;
ears that can hear will actually listen.
32.4:
The heart of the reckless will understand at last,
and tongues that once stumbled and stammered
will speak with clarity.
32.5:
The fool will no longer be called noble;
the miser will no longer be praised as generous.
32.6:
For the fool speaks foolishness;
his heart brews nothing but wickedness —
practicing profanity and preaching distortion about Yahweh,
leaving the hungry with empty hands,
denying water to those who are parched.
32.7:
The miser's tools are all evil —
he schemes with wicked designs
to ruin the poor with lies,
even when the needy speaks justly.
32.8:
But the one with a generous heart devises generous acts —
and by those generous acts he stands.
32.10:
Within a year and a little more,
you who are so complacent will shudder —
for the grape harvest will fail
and the gathering will not come.
32.11:
Tremble, you women at ease;
shudder, you who feel so secure!
Strip off your fine clothes,
lay yourselves bare,
and tie on sackcloth at your waist.
32.13:
Thorns and briers will overgrow
my people's land —
yes, every house of celebration
in the city once filled with joy.
32.14:
The palace will be abandoned,
the crowded city emptied out;
the citadel and the watchtower will become
forever dens for wild donkeys,
pasture for wandering flocks —
32.15:
until the Spirit is poured upon us from on high.
Then the wilderness will become an orchard,
and what is now orchard will seem like ancient forest.
32.16:
Then justice will settle in the wilderness,
and righteousness make its permanent home in the orchard.
32.18:
My people will live in peaceful homes,
in secure dwellings,
in undisturbed resting places —
32.19:
even when hail comes crashing through the forest
and the city is leveled to the ground.
32.20:
Blessed are you who sow
beside every flowing stream —
who let the ox and donkey
range freely where they will.

33.1:
Woe to you, destroyer — you who have not yet been destroyed!
You who betray — and have not been betrayed!
When you stop destroying, you will be destroyed;
when you finish with betrayal, you will be betrayed.
33.2:
O Yahweh, be gracious to us —
we have waited for you.
Be our arm of strength every morning,
our salvation in the time of trouble.
33.3:
At the thunder of your voice the peoples flee;
at your rising up the nations scatter.
33.4:
Your plunder will be stripped away
as locusts strip a field —
people rushing over it like a swarm of grasshoppers.
33.5:
Yahweh is exalted — he dwells on high;
he has filled Zion with justice and righteousness.
33.6:
He will be the steady foundation beneath your days:
salvation in abundance, wisdom, and knowledge —
and the fear of Yahweh is the treasure he offers.
33.8:
The highways lie empty, all travel stopped.
He broke the covenant,
he despised the cities,
he has no regard for any human life.
33.12:
The peoples will be burned to lime —
like cut thornbushes thrown into the fire.
33.14:
The sinners in Zion are terrified;
trembling seizes the godless:
'Who among us can live next to the devouring fire?
Who among us can survive among everlasting flames?'
33.15:
The one who walks in righteousness
and speaks honestly,
who despises profit squeezed from the poor,
who shakes bribe-money from his hands,
who stops his ears from hearing of bloodshed,
who shuts his eyes rather than gaze on evil —
33.17:
Your eyes will see the king in his beauty —
you will look out over a land
that stretches far in every direction.
33.18:
Your heart will look back on the old terror and ask —
'Where is the scribe now?
Where is the one who counted out the tribute?
Where is the one who tallied the towers?'
33.19:
You will see that brutal people no more —
that people with speech too deep to follow,
with a strange, garbled tongue
you could never understand.
33.20:
Look upon Zion —
city of our sacred assemblies!
Your eyes will see Jerusalem:
a dwelling at peace,
a tent that will never be taken down,
whose stakes will never be pulled up,
whose ropes will never snap.
33.21:
But there Yahweh in his glory
will be for us a place of wide rivers and flowing streams —
no warship with oars will sail there,
no great vessel will pass through.
33.23:
Your ship's ropes hang loose —
the mast cannot be held upright,
no sail can be unfurled.
Then the plunder will be divided,
and even the lame will take their share of the spoil.
33.24:
And no one living there will say, 'I am sick' —
the people who dwell in that city
will have their iniquity forgiven.

34.1:
Draw near, every nation — hear this!
Listen, every people!
Let the earth hear, and all that fills it —
the world and everything that comes out of it.
34.2:
Yahweh's indignation falls on every nation,
his fury on every one of their armies.
He has devoted them to destruction —
given them over to slaughter.
34.3:
Their corpses will be thrown out unburied —
the stench rising from their bodies —
and the mountains will run with their blood.
34.4:
The whole army of heaven will dissolve —
the sky rolled up like a scroll —
all their host falling
like a leaf stripped from the vine,
like a fig dropping from a fig tree.
Creation runs in reverse.
34.5:
My sword has been soaking in heaven —
see how it descends on Edom,
on the people I have consigned to destruction.
34.6:
Yahweh's sword is full of blood,
dripping with fat —
blood of lambs and goats,
fat of rams' kidneys.
For Yahweh holds a sacrifice in Bozrah,
a great slaughter in the land of Edom.
34.7:
Wild oxen go down alongside them,
the young bulls with the great bulls —
the land soaked with blood,
the soil made rich with fat.
34.8:
For Yahweh has his day of vengeance —
a year of reckoning for Zion's cause.
34.9:
The streams of Edom will turn to tar,
its dust to sulfur —
the land itself a lake of burning pitch.
34.10:
Never quenched — night or day —
its smoke rising forever.
From generation to generation, desolate;
no one will ever cross it again.
34.11:
Pelican and hedgehog will inherit it;
owl and raven will nest there.
Yahweh will stretch over it
the surveyor's line of chaos
and the plumb line of emptiness.
34.12:
The title they will give it: No Kingdom Here.
All its nobles — gone to nothing.
34.13:
Thorns will take over its palaces,
nettles and brambles its fortresses —
it will become the jackal's lair,
a roost for ostriches.
34.14:
Wildcats and hyenas will meet in its ruins;
wild goats will call to each other.
The night owl will make her home there
and find her rest.
34.15:
There the great owl nests —
laying eggs, hatching,
gathering her young under her shadow —
and the hawks assemble, each with its mate.
34.16:
Search the book of Yahweh and read:
not one of these will be missing,
not one will lack its mate —
for my mouth has commanded it,
and his Spirit has assembled them.
34.17:
He has cast the lot for them;
his hand has apportioned it by the measuring line.
They will possess it forever —
from generation to generation, they will dwell there.

35.1:
The wilderness and the parched land will burst with joy;
the desert will celebrate and bloom like the crocus.
35.2:
It will burst into bloom —
overflow with joy and singing.
The glory of Lebanon given to it,
the splendor of Carmel and Sharon —
and they will see the glory of Yahweh,
the majesty of our God.
35.3:
Strengthen the weak hands;
steady the shaking knees.
35.4:
Say to those whose hearts are trembling with fear:
'Be strong — do not be afraid!
Look — your God is coming,
coming with vengeance, with divine reckoning.
He will come and he will save you.'
35.5:
Then blind eyes will open
and deaf ears be cleared.
35.6:
The lame will leap like a deer;
the silent tongue will break into song.
For water will burst out in the wilderness
and streams cut through the desert.
35.7:
Shimmering desert mirages will become actual pools;
parched ground will spring up with water.
Where jackals once lay in the waste,
grass and reeds and rushes will grow.
35.8:
A highway will be there — a road —
called the Way of Holiness.
No one unclean will walk on it.
It is for those who travel it;
even those who cannot find their way
will not go astray on it.
35.9:
No lion on that road,
no ferocious animal will set foot on it —
none will be found there.
Only the redeemed will walk it.
35.10:
The ransomed of Yahweh will return —
they will arrive at Zion with songs,
everlasting joy crowning their heads.
Gladness and joy will overtake them;
sorrow and sighing will run away.

36.1: In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah's reign, Sennacherib king of Assyria marched against every fortified city of Judah and took them.
36.2: Then the king of Assyria sent his field commander Rabshakeh from Lachish to King Hezekiah in Jerusalem with a massive force. He stationed himself at the channel of the upper pool on the road to the Washerman's Field.
36.3: Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace administrator, went out to him, along with Shebna the secretary and Joah son of Asaph, the royal recorder.
36.4: Rabshakeh said to them, 'Take this message to Hezekiah: This is what the great king — the king of Assyria — says: What is all this confidence you keep leaning on?'
36.5: 'Let me tell you — words alone are not military strategy or battle strength. Who exactly are you counting on, that you have dared to rebel against me?'
36.6: 'You are leaning on Egypt — that cracked, broken reed of a staff. Press on it and it drives through your hand. That is exactly what Pharaoh king of Egypt is to anyone who trusts him.'
36.7: 'Perhaps you'll say, "We trust in the LORD our God." But isn't that the very God whose high places and altars Hezekiah tore down, commanding Judah and Jerusalem to worship only at this one altar?'
36.8: 'Come then — make a deal with my master the king of Assyria. I will give you two thousand horses — if you can find enough men to ride them.'
36.9: 'How then can you possibly face down even the most junior officer in my master's army? Yet here you are, counting on Egypt for chariots and cavalry!'
36.10: 'Do you think I came against this land without the LORD's say-so? Yahweh himself told me: Go up against this land and destroy it.'
36.11: Then Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah said to Rabshakeh, 'Please — speak to us in Aramaic. We understand it perfectly. Don't speak Hebrew where everyone on the wall can hear.'
36.12: But Rabshakeh answered: 'Did my master send this message only to your master and to you? Was it not sent just as much to the people sitting on the wall — who will end up eating their own filth and drinking their own urine when this is finished?'
36.13: Then Rabshakeh stood up and bellowed in Hebrew at the top of his voice: 'Listen! Hear the words of the great king — the king of Assyria!'
36.14: 'This is the king's message: Don't let Hezekiah deceive you. He cannot save you.'
36.15: 'And do not let Hezekiah talk you into trusting Yahweh by saying, "Yahweh will definitely rescue us — this city will never fall to the king of Assyria."'
36.16: 'Stop listening to Hezekiah. Here is what the king of Assyria offers: Make peace with me and come out — every one of you will eat from your own vine and fig tree and drink from your own well,'
36.17: '— until I come and take you to a land just like this one: grain and wine, bread and vineyards.'
36.18: 'Be warned: don't let Hezekiah seduce you with talk about Yahweh rescuing you. Has any nation's god ever rescued his country from the king of Assyria?'
36.19: 'Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did any of them rescue Samaria from me?'
36.20: 'Name one — name one god of any of these nations who delivered his land from me. So why should Yahweh deliver Jerusalem?'
36.21: They said nothing. Not a word. Because Hezekiah had given the order: Do not answer him.
36.22: Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace administrator, Shebna the secretary, and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder, went to Hezekiah with their robes torn and told him everything Rabshakeh had said.

37.1: The moment King Hezekiah heard the news, he tore his clothes, wrapped himself in sackcloth, and went straight to the house of Yahweh.
37.2: He dispatched Eliakim the palace administrator, Shebna the royal secretary, and the senior priests — all wrapped in sackcloth — to the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz.
37.3: They said to him: 'This is Hezekiah's word: Today is a day of catastrophe, of rebuke, of contempt. We are like a woman who has reached the birth canal with no strength left to push.'
37.4: Perhaps Yahweh your God has heard every insult Rabshakeh hurled against the living God on his master's orders — and will answer those words with judgment. He is your God; lift up your prayer for the few who still remain.
37.5: The servants of King Hezekiah came to Isaiah.
37.6: Isaiah told them: 'Tell your master this — Yahweh says: Do not be afraid of the words that have reached you, the insults the servants of Assyria's king have hurled at me.'
37.7: Watch what I do: I will send a disturbing report into his ear, and he will turn back to his own land — and there I will bring him down by the sword.
37.8: Rabshakeh withdrew and found the king of Assyria engaged at Libnah — the king had moved up from Lachish.
37.9: Then word reached him about Tirhakah king of Cush: 'He has come out to fight you.' When Sennacherib heard it, he sent another message to Hezekiah:
37.10: 'Tell Hezekiah king of Judah: Do not let the God you are trusting fool you with the promise that Jerusalem will be spared from the king of Assyria.'
37.11: 'You have heard what Assyria's kings have done to every land they touched — total destruction. Do you imagine you will escape?'
37.12: 'Where were the gods of those nations when my fathers destroyed them — Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, the people of Eden in Telassar? Not one of their gods did a thing.'
37.13: 'Where is the king of Hamath now? Where is the king of Arphad? Where are the kings of Sepharvaim, Hena, and Ivah?'
37.14: Hezekiah took the letter from the messengers and read it. Then he went up to the house of Yahweh and spread it open before Yahweh.
37.16: 'O Yahweh of hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim — you, you alone, are God over all the kingdoms of the earth. You made heaven and earth.
37.17: Bend down, Yahweh — hear us. Open your eyes and see. Listen to every word Sennacherib has sent to insult the living God.
37.18: It is true, Yahweh — Assyria's kings have destroyed nation after nation, land after land,
37.19: and thrown their gods into the fire — because those so-called gods were nothing, things men made from wood and stone; of course they could be burned.
37.20: So now, Yahweh our God, rescue us from his grip — so that every kingdom on earth will know that you, Yahweh, are God, and you alone.'
37.21: Then Isaiah son of Amoz sent a message to Hezekiah: 'Yahweh, the God of Israel, says this: Your prayer about Sennacherib king of Assyria has been heard.
37.22:
'This is what Yahweh says about him:
The virgin daughter of Zion despises you —
she laughs you to scorn.
Daughter Jerusalem shakes her head as you flee.
37.23:
Whom did you insult? Whom did you blaspheme?
Against whom did you raise your voice,
lifting your eyes high in arrogance?
Against the Holy One of Israel.
37.24:
Through your servants you mocked the Lord — you boasted:
'With my vast chariots I climbed the peaks of the mountains,
the far reaches of Lebanon.
I cut down its tallest cedars, its finest cypresses.
I reached the summit of its heights,
the thickest depths of its forests.'
37.26:
'Have you not heard?
Long ago I drew up this plan;
I formed it in ancient days.
Now I have carried it out —
you are only the instrument
by which I reduce fortified cities to rubble.'
37.27:
'Their people had no real strength —
shattered, humiliated,
like field plants, like tender shoots,
like rooftop grass that scorches before it can grow,
blasted before it reaches its height.'
37.28:
'But I know where you live.
I know your coming and going.
I know your raging against me.'
37.29:
'Because your raging and your insolence
have reached my ears,
I will put my hook in your nose
and my bridle between your lips —
and drag you back the way you came.'
37.30:
'And this is your sign:
This year you eat what grows wild;
next year, what springs up on its own.
But the third year — sow and reap,
plant vineyards, eat their fruit again.'
37.31:
'The surviving remnant of the house of Judah
will take root again in the soil
and bear fruit toward the sky —'
37.32:
'for from Jerusalem a remnant will come forth,
a company of survivors from Mount Zion.
The zeal of Yahweh of hosts will bring this to pass.'
37.33:
Therefore Yahweh says about the king of Assyria:
'He will not set foot in this city.
He will not fire a single arrow into it.
He will not come at it with a shield.
He will not build a siege ramp against it.'
37.34: 'By the road he came, by that road he goes back. He will not enter this city.' Yahweh's own word.
37.35: 'I will defend this city and save it — for my own sake and for the sake of my servant David.'
37.36: That night the angel of Yahweh went out and struck down a hundred and eighty-five thousand in the Assyrian camp. When the survivors woke at dawn, there were nothing but dead bodies everywhere.
37.37: Sennacherib king of Assyria broke camp and went home to Nineveh.
37.38: Then while he was at worship in the temple of his god Nisroch, his own sons Adrammelech and Sharezer killed him with the sword and fled to Ararat. His son Esarhaddon succeeded him as king.

38.1: In those same days Hezekiah fell deathly sick. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him and said: 'Yahweh says this: Set your house in order. You are going to die — you will not recover.'
38.2: Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to Yahweh.
38.3: He prayed: 'Remember, Yahweh — please — how I have walked before you in truth and with an undivided heart, and done what is good in your eyes.' And Hezekiah wept and wept.
38.4: Then the word of Yahweh came to Isaiah:
38.5: 'Go, tell Hezekiah: Yahweh says — I, the God of your father David — I have heard your prayer. I have seen your tears. I am adding fifteen years to your life.'
38.6: 'I will rescue you and this city from the king of Assyria. I will defend this city.'
38.7: 'And this is the sign Yahweh gives you to confirm that he will do what he has promised:'
38.8: 'I will reverse the shadow on the sundial of Ahaz — those ten steps it has already descended — ten steps backward.' And the sun went back the ten steps it had gone down.
38.9: The writing of Hezekiah king of Judah — composed after his illness, when he recovered:
38.10:
I said:
In the noontide of my years I must go —
consigned to the gates of Sheol,
my remaining years stripped away.
38.11:
I said:
I will never see Yahweh again —
never, in the land of the living.
I will look on human faces no more
among those who walk in this world.
38.12:
My home is struck and gone —
pulled up like a shepherd's tent.
Like a weaver I have rolled up my life;
he cuts me from the loom.
From morning to night you are finishing me.
38.13:
I endured till morning —
but like a lion he was grinding my bones.
From morning to night you were finishing me.
38.14:
Like a swift, like a crane —
that was the sound of my crying.
I moaned like a dove.
My eyes grew weary straining upward.
Yahweh, I am crushed — be my surety.
38.15:
What can I say?
He spoke — and he himself brought it to pass.
I will walk quietly, weighed down,
all my years under the bitterness of my soul.
38.16:
O Lord, it is by such things —
these hard things — that people truly live.
In all of this is the life of my spirit.
Restore me; let me live.
38.17:
I see it now:
that bitterness was for my good.
You pulled my life back from the pit —
you loved me enough to do that —
and you have thrown every one of my sins
behind your back, where you cannot see them.
38.18:
For Sheol cannot praise you.
Death cannot give you thanks.
Those who go down to the pit
cannot hope in your faithfulness.
38.19:
The living — only the living — give you thanks.
As I do today.
A father tells his children of your faithfulness.
38.20:
Yahweh was ready to save me —
and we will sing my praise songs
with strings ringing out
all the days of our lives
in the house of Yahweh.
38.21: Now Isaiah had said: 'Take a poultice of pressed figs and lay it on the sore — he will recover.'
38.22: And Hezekiah had asked: 'What will be the sign that I can go up to the house of Yahweh?'

39.1: At that time Merodach-baladan son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent envoys with letters and a gift to Hezekiah. He had heard that Hezekiah had been sick and recovered.
39.2: Hezekiah welcomed them eagerly and showed them everything — his treasury, his silver and gold, his spices and precious oils, his whole armory, everything in all his storehouses. There was not a single thing in his palace or anywhere in his kingdom that he did not put on display.
39.3: Then the prophet Isaiah came to King Hezekiah and asked: 'What did those men say? Where did they come from?' Hezekiah answered: 'They came from far away — from Babylon.'
39.4: 'What have they seen in your palace?' Isaiah pressed him. 'Everything,' Hezekiah said. 'There is nothing in my storehouses I did not show them.'
39.5: Then Isaiah said: 'Hear the word of Yahweh of hosts:'
39.6: 'Days are coming when everything in this palace — everything your ancestors accumulated down to this very day — will be carried off to Babylon. Nothing will be left. Yahweh says so.'
39.7: 'And some of the sons born to you — your own flesh — will be taken away and become servants in the palace of Babylon's king.'
39.8: Hezekiah said to Isaiah: 'The word of Yahweh you have spoken is good.' And then he added: 'At least there will be peace and security in my own days.'

43.1:
But now — hear this, Jacob;
listen, Israel, whom God formed:
Do not be afraid.
I have redeemed you.
I called you by your own name —
you are mine.
43.2:
When you wade through the waters, I will be there;
the rivers will not sweep you away.
When you walk through the fire,
you will not be burned —
not a flame will touch you.
43.3:
For I am Yahweh your God,
the Holy One of Israel — your Savior.
I give Egypt as the price of your rescue,
Ethiopia and Seba in exchange for you.
43.4:
Because you are precious to me,
because I hold you in honor,
because I love you —
I will give up whole nations for you,
peoples in exchange for your life.
43.5:
Do not be afraid — I am with you.
I will bring your children back from the east
and gather you from the west.
43.6:
I will command the north: Let them go!
I will tell the south: Don't hold them back!
Bring my sons from far away —
bring my daughters from the ends of the earth —
43.7:
everyone who carries my name,
everyone I created for my glory,
everyone I formed and made.
43.8:
Bring out the people who have eyes but cannot see,
who have ears but cannot hear.
43.9:
Let every nation gather,
let every people assemble.
Who among them predicted this?
Who announced what came before?
Let them bring their witnesses to make their case —
or let them hear and admit: it is true.
43.10:
'You are my witnesses,' declares Yahweh —
'you and my servant whom I chose —
so that you would know this, trust this, understand:
I am the one.
Before me there was no god formed;
after me — none.'
43.11:
I — I alone — am Yahweh.
Apart from me, there is no one who saves.
43.12:
I was the one who declared it.
I was the one who saved.
I proclaimed it —
when no foreign god was among you.
'You are my witnesses,' declares Yahweh.
'And I am God.'
43.13:
Before created time began, I was.
No one can snatch anything from my hand.
When I act — who undoes what I do?
43.14:
Here is what Yahweh says —
your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel:
'For your sake I have sent against Babylon
and will drive all the Chaldeans down as refugees —
into the very ships they once prided themselves on.'
43.15:
I am Yahweh, your Holy One,
Creator of Israel,
your King.
43.16:
Here is what Yahweh says —
he who once opened a road through the sea
and a path through the raging waters,
43.17:
who drove out chariot and horse,
army and champion together —
they lay down and could not rise again,
snuffed out like a burning wick:
43.18:
'Stop dwelling on what happened before.
Stop thinking about the old days.'
43.19:
'Look — I am doing something completely new.
Even now it is breaking through — do you see it?
I am opening a road through the wilderness
and rivers through the barren desert.'
43.20:
Even the wild animals will honor me —
the desert jackals and the ostriches —
because I bring water into the wilderness
and rivers into the desert,
to quench the thirst of my chosen people,
43.21:
the people I shaped for myself —
so they would tell everyone what I have done.
43.22:
And yet, Jacob — you never called on me.
Israel — you grew tired of me.
43.23:
You brought me no sheep for burnt offerings.
Your sacrifices brought me no honor.
I never loaded you down with offerings —
never wore you out with demands for incense.
43.24:
You spent no silver to buy me sweet cane;
you did not satisfy me with the fat of your offerings.
But you have loaded me down with your sins —
worn me out with your rebellions.
43.25:
And yet — I, I myself am the one
who wipes away your rebellions —
for my own sake, not yours.
I will not hold your sins against you.
43.26:
Remind me, then — let us take this to court.
Present your case.
Make your argument.
See if you can be acquitted.
43.27:
Your first father sinned.
Every representative you ever had has betrayed me.
43.28:
So I stripped the sanctuary's leaders of their dignity
and gave Jacob over to total destruction —
Israel to contempt and insult.

44.1:
Even so — listen now, Jacob my servant.
Israel — you whom I chose — hear this.
44.2:
Here is what Yahweh says —
he who made you, who shaped you before you were born,
who will sustain you:
'Do not fear, Jacob my servant,
Jeshurun whom I chose.'
44.3:
For I will pour water on the parched land
and floods on the dry ground.
I will pour out my Spirit on your children —
my blessing on those who come after you.
44.4:
They will rise up like grass in a meadow,
like willows planted by running water.
44.5:
One person will say, 'I belong to Yahweh.'
Another will take on the name of Jacob.
Another will write on his hand: 'Yahweh's' —
and adopt the name Israel as his own.
44.6:
Here is what Yahweh says —
Israel's King and Kinsman-Redeemer,
Yahweh of armies:
'I am the first. I am the last.
Between those two, there is no other God.'
44.7:
Who is my equal? Let him step forward and say so.
Let him declare and set it out before me:
who has announced events from the beginning,
as I have since I first set this people in place?
Let them tell what is coming — what is still ahead.
44.8:
Do not tremble. Do not be afraid.
Have I not been saying this all along —
declaring it from the beginning?
You yourselves are my witnesses:
Is there any God besides me?
There is no Rock — none that I know of.
44.9:
All idol-makers amount to nothing.
The things they prize so highly — useless, every one.
Their own witnesses are blind and ignorant.
They will be disgraced.
44.10: Who would go to the trouble of shaping a god — casting a metal image that does nothing for anyone?
44.11: All who are part of this trade will be shamed. The craftsmen who make idols — they are only human. Let them all come together and take a stand. They will be terrified. They will be disgraced. Every last one.
44.12: The blacksmith heats the iron in his forge, beats it into shape with his hammer, works it with his powerful arms. But he grows hungry and loses his strength. If he forgets to drink, he faints.
44.13: The carpenter takes his measuring line, chalks it out, works the wood with chisels, traces curves with his compass — and slowly it takes the form of a person, a beautiful human figure, to be placed inside a house.
44.14: He goes out and cuts down cedars — or chooses a cypress, an oak — lets them grow strong in the forest. He planted it. The rain made it grow.
44.15: Then that same tree becomes his fuel. He burns part of it. Warms himself. Bakes his bread. And then — with another piece — he carves a god. He bows down to it. He worships it.
44.16: Half he burns in the fire. Over the coals he roasts his meat, eats his fill. He warms himself and says, 'Good — I can feel the warmth of the fire.'
44.17: And then, with what is left, he makes his god — a carved image. He bows down to it. He worships it. He prays to it: 'Save me — you are my god!'
44.18: They do not know. They do not understand. Their eyes have been sealed shut so they cannot see. Their hearts have been closed so they cannot grasp what they are doing.
44.19: No one pauses to think. No one has the insight or the will to say: 'I just burned half this tree to roast my dinner — baked bread on its coals, ate my fill. And now I am going to make the rest into an idol? Bow down to a piece of wood?'
44.20: He is eating ashes and doesn't know it. A deceived heart has led him off the path. He cannot rescue himself — cannot ask the honest question: 'Is what I am holding in my hand a lie?'
44.21:
Remember all this, Jacob.
Keep it in mind, Israel —
you are my servant.
I formed you. You belong to me.
Israel — I will not forget you.
44.22:
I have swept your rebellions away like a morning mist —
your sins like a cloud scattered by the wind.
Return to me —
for I have redeemed you.
44.23:
Heavens, shout for joy — Yahweh has done it!
Earth, shout from your depths!
Mountains, forests, every tree — break into singing!
For Yahweh has redeemed Jacob
and shown his glory in Israel.
44.24:
Here is what Yahweh says —
your Redeemer, who shaped you before birth:
'I am Yahweh, maker of all things.
I alone stretched out the sky.
I spread out the earth — by myself —'
44.25:
'who exposes the signs of charlatans as frauds,
who makes diviners look foolish,
who reverses the thinking of the wise
and reduces their knowledge to nonsense,'
44.27:
'who says to the deep: Dry up!
who says to the rivers: I will drain you dry,'
44.28:
'who says of Cyrus: he is my shepherd —
he will carry out everything I intend.
He will say of Jerusalem: rebuild it.
He will say of the temple: lay its foundation.'

40.1:
Comfort, comfort my people —
says your God.
40.2:
Speak to Jerusalem's heart — announce to her:
her hard service is over,
her guilt is pardoned.
From Yahweh's own hand she has received
double payment for all her sins.
40.3:
A voice cries in the wilderness:
Prepare the way of Yahweh!
Make straight through the desert
a highway for our God.
40.4:
Every valley will be raised,
every mountain and hill brought low —
crooked terrain made level,
rugged ground a smooth plain.
40.5:
The glory of Yahweh will be unveiled,
and all humanity will see it together —
for the mouth of Yahweh has spoken.
40.6:
A voice says: Cry!
What shall I cry? he asked.
All humanity is grass;
all its glory is like a wildflower.
40.7:
Grass withers, the flower fades
when the breath of Yahweh blows over them —
yes, the people are grass.
40.8:
Grass withers, flowers fade —
but the word of our God
stands forever.
40.9:
Climb the high mountain, O Zion —
you who carry this good news!
Shout without fear, O Jerusalem —
you who herald this good news!
Call out to the cities of Judah:
Look — your God is here!
40.11:
He tends his flock like a shepherd —
gathering the lambs in his arms,
carrying them against his chest,
gently leading the nursing mothers.
40.12:
Who has scooped the oceans into his palm,
measured the heavens with a handspan,
packed the earth's dust into a cup,
weighed mountains on a scale
and hills in a balance?
40.13:
Who has directed the Spirit of Yahweh?
Who has served as his counselor to instruct him?
40.14:
Who was his advisor?
Who taught him how to act justly?
Who gave him knowledge
or showed him the way of understanding?
40.15:
The nations are a drop in a bucket to him —
weighed on his scales they are dust.
He picks up the coastlands
like a pinch of fine powder.
40.16:
All of Lebanon's forests would not suffice for fuel,
nor would all its animals make an adequate burnt offering.
40.17:
Before him, every nation is nothing —
less than nothing he counts them,
a breath of vapor.
40.18:
So to whom will you compare God?
What image could come close?
40.19:
A craftsman casts an idol.
The goldsmith plates it with gold,
shaping silver chains to adorn it.
40.20:
A man too poor for a proper offering
chooses a wood that will not rot;
he hunts for a skilled craftsman
to set up an idol that will not fall.
40.21:
Don't you know?
Haven't you heard?
Hasn't this been told you from the very beginning?
Haven't you understood from the moment the earth was founded?
40.22:
He is the one who sits enthroned above the circle of the earth,
whose inhabitants look up like grasshoppers —
who stretches out the heavens like a curtain
and spreads them like a tent to live in.
40.23:
He reduces princes to nothing
and makes earth's rulers vanish like vapor.
40.24:
Barely planted, barely sown —
their roots have barely taken hold —
he breathes on them and they wither.
The whirlwind carries them off like stubble.
40.25:
'So — to whom will you compare me?
Who is my equal?' says the Holy One.
40.26:
Lift your eyes up and look:
Who created all these?
He marches out their ranks by number,
calling each one by name.
So great is his power and mighty his strength
that not one of them goes missing.
40.27:
Why do you say this, Jacob —
why do you insist, Israel —
'My path is hidden from Yahweh;
my God takes no notice of my case'?
40.28:
Don't you know?
Haven't you heard?
Yahweh is the everlasting God —
Creator of the far ends of the earth.
He never grows faint or weary;
his understanding no one can fathom.
40.29:
He gives power to the weary
and multiplies strength for those who have none.
40.30:
Even young men grow faint and weary;
even the strong ones stumble and collapse —
40.31:
but those who wait on Yahweh will renew their strength.
They will soar on wings like eagles;
they will run and not grow weary,
they will walk and not faint.

41.1:
Fall silent before me, O coastlands;
let the peoples gather their strength.
Let them come forward — let them speak.
Let us come near together for the verdict.
41.3:
He pursues, passes on, unharmed —
across terrain his feet have never traveled.
41.4:
Who has done all this,
calling the generations into being from the beginning?
I, Yahweh — I am the first,
and with the last I am the same. I am he.
41.6:
Each craftsman encourages his fellow;
brother says to brother, 'Hold firm!'
41.7:
Craftsman cheers on goldsmith;
the one who hammers smooth encourages
the one who strikes the anvil:
'The join is solid!' they say.
They nail it down so it cannot move.
41.8:
But you, Israel — you are my servant,
Jacob, whom I have chosen,
offspring of Abraham, my friend —
41.9:
you whom I took from the ends of the earth
and called from its farthest corners,
saying: You are my servant,
I have chosen you and not cast you aside.
41.10:
Do not fear — I am with you.
Do not be dismayed — I am your God.
I will strengthen you, I will help you,
I will hold you up with my righteous right hand.
41.11:
Every one who rages against you
will end in shame and disgrace.
Those who fight you will come to nothing —
they will perish.
41.12:
You will look for those who fight you
and not find them.
Those who make war on you
will be nothing — utterly gone.
41.13:
For I, Yahweh your God, am gripping your right hand,
saying to you: Do not fear — I am the one who helps you.
41.14:
Do not be afraid, worm Jacob —
you who feel so small, O people of Israel!
I am your helper — Yahweh declares it.
Your Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel.
41.15:
Look — I will make you a new threshing sledge,
sharp with many teeth.
You will thresh the mountains and grind them to dust;
you will reduce the hills to chaff.
41.17:
When the poor and needy seek water
and there is none,
when their tongues are cracked with thirst —
I, Yahweh, will answer them;
I, the God of Israel, will not abandon them.
41.18:
I will open rivers on the bare heights
and springs in the valley floors.
I will turn the wilderness into a standing pool
and the parched ground into flowing springs.
41.19:
In the wilderness I will plant
cedar, acacia, myrtle, and olive.
In the desert I will set
cypress, plane, and pine side by side —
41.20:
— so that all may see and know,
consider and understand together,
that the hand of Yahweh has done this,
that the Holy One of Israel has created it.
41.21:
State your case — Yahweh demands it.
Bring your strongest arguments —
says the King of Jacob.
41.22:
Let them step forward and tell us what will happen.
Let them explain the former things — what they were —
so we can trace where they led.
Or let them announce what is coming.
41.23:
Foretell what is coming, so we may know
you actually are gods.
Do something — good or bad —
so we may stare at it and tremble.
41.24:
You are nothing.
Your works are nothing.
Whoever chooses you is revolting.
41.25:
I have roused one from the north — he comes;
from the east he calls on my name.
He treads on rulers as on mud,
tramples them as a potter works his clay.
41.26:
Who declared this beforehand, so we could know?
Who foretold it, so we could say, 'Right!'?
No one declared it.
No one announced it.
No one heard a word from you.
41.27:
I was the first to announce to Zion:
Look — here they come!
I am giving Jerusalem one who brings the good news.
41.28:
I looked — there was no one.
Among all these, not a counselor to be found.
I asked, and not one of them could answer.
41.29:
There they all are — vapor, every one.
Their works: nothing.
Their cast images: wind and confusion.

42.1:
Here is my servant, whom I uphold —
my chosen one, in whom my heart delights.
I have placed my Spirit upon him;
he will bring forth justice to the nations.
42.2:
He will not shout or raise his voice
or make himself heard in the streets.
42.4:
He will not falter or lose heart
until he has planted justice in the earth —
and the coastlands wait in hope for his instruction.
42.5:
This is what God Yahweh says —
he who created the heavens and stretched them out,
who spread out the earth and all that grows from it,
who gives breath to the people on it
and spirit to those who walk on it:
42.6:
I, Yahweh, have called you in righteousness;
I have taken hold of your hand and I will keep you.
I will make you a covenant for the people,
a light for the nations —
42.7:
to open eyes that are blind,
to bring out prisoners from the dungeon,
to release from the dark cell those who have been bound.
42.8:
I am Yahweh — that is my name.
My glory I will not share with anyone.
My praise I will not give to carved idols.
42.9:
The former things have come to pass —
new things I now declare.
Before they spring up, I am telling you.
42.10:
Sing to Yahweh a new song —
his praise from the ends of the earth!
You who sail the seas and all that fills them,
you coastlands and all who live on them.
42.11:
Let the wilderness and its settlements shout,
the villages where Kedar's people live.
Let the people of Sela sing for joy;
let them shout from the mountain peaks.
42.12:
Let them give glory to Yahweh
and proclaim his praise in the coastlands.
42.13:
Yahweh marches out like a warrior,
like a soldier of war he fans his fury.
He shouts — he roars —
he prevails over his enemies.
42.14:
For so long I have been silent,
still, holding myself back.
But now I will cry out like a woman in labor —
gasping, panting.
42.15:
I will lay mountains and hills waste,
drying up all their vegetation.
I will make rivers into dry land
and drain the pools.
42.16:
I will lead the blind on roads they have never walked,
guide them on paths they have never known.
Darkness I will turn into light before them;
rough terrain into level ground.
These things I will do —
I will not abandon them.
42.17:
Those who trust in carved idols,
who say to cast images, 'You are our gods' —
they will be driven back in utter disgrace.
42.18:
Hear, you who are deaf!
Look, you who are blind — see!
42.19:
Who is as blind as my servant?
As deaf as the messenger I send?
Blind as the one I have set apart —
blind as Yahweh's own servant?
42.20:
Seeing much but noticing nothing;
ears open but not listening.
42.21:
It was Yahweh's pleasure, for the sake of his own righteousness,
to make his law great and magnificent.
42.22:
But this is a people robbed and stripped —
all of them caught in holes,
hidden in dungeons.
Made prey, and no one rescues.
Made plunder, and no one says, 'Give them back.'
42.23:
Who among you will truly listen to this?
Who will pay close attention for what is coming?
42.24:
Who handed Jacob over to robbers?
Who gave Israel up to plunderers?
Was it not Yahweh — he against whom we sinned?
They refused to walk in his ways;
they would not obey his law.
42.25:
So Yahweh poured his fury and the force of war upon him —
set him ablaze on every side —
but he did not understand.
Burned — and he never took it to heart.

45.1: Yahweh says this to Cyrus — his anointed, his chosen instrument — the one whose right hand Yahweh himself has taken hold of, to flatten nations before him and strip kings of their defenses, to throw open every door so that no gate stays shut:
45.2: I will march before you and level every obstacle. Bronze gates — I will shatter them. Iron bars — I will cut through them.
45.3: I will hand you the treasures stashed away in darkness, the riches hidden in secret vaults — all so that you may come to know that I, Yahweh, the God of Israel, am the one who called you by name.
45.4: I did it for the sake of Jacob my servant — for Israel my chosen. I called you by name, I gave you your title — though you had no knowledge of me at all.
45.5: I am Yahweh, and there is no other. No god exists beside me. I armed you for this — though you never knew me —
45.6: so that from sunrise to sunset every people on earth may come to know there is no one beside me. I am Yahweh — and there is no other.
45.7: I shape light and create darkness; I make peace and bring disaster. I, Yahweh, do all these things.
45.8: Rain down from above, O heavens — let the skies shower righteousness. Let the earth open wide and salvation spring up; let righteousness spring up alongside it. I, Yahweh, have created all of this.
45.9: Woe to the one who fights with the one who made him — a clay shard demanding answers from other shards. Does the clay interrogate the potter: 'What do you think you're making?' or 'You left it without handles'?
45.10: Woe to the one who demands of a father, 'Why are you having this child?' or says to a woman in labor, 'Why are you giving birth to this?'
45.11: Yahweh says this — the Holy One of Israel, the one who formed him: 'Are you going to interrogate me about my own children? Are you going to issue orders about what my hands have made?'
45.12: I made this earth. I created humanity on it. With these hands I stretched out the sky, and I issued the command that set every star in its place.
45.13: I roused Cyrus in my own righteous purpose, and I will level every road before him. He will rebuild my city and release my exiles — not for payment, not for a bribe. Yahweh of hosts says so.
45.14: Yahweh says: Egypt's wealth and the trade of Cush, and the tall men of Seba — they will all come over to you, yours by right. They will walk behind you in chains, bowing down, and plead: 'Surely God is with you — and there is no one else, no other god at all.'
45.15: You are indeed a God who hides himself — O God of Israel, the one who saves.
45.16: They are all shamed and humiliated, together. Every maker of idols walks away in disgrace.
45.17: But Israel — Israel is saved by Yahweh with a salvation that lasts forever. You will never again be ashamed or humiliated — not for ages without end.
45.18: For Yahweh says this — the one who created the heavens (he is God), the one who shaped the earth and made it, who set it in place, who did not create it as a void but formed it to be lived in: I am Yahweh, and there is no other.
45.19: I never spoke in secret, from some dark hidden place. I never told Jacob's children, 'Look for me in the void.' I, Yahweh, speak what is true; I declare what is right.
45.20: Come together and gather — draw near, all you who survived among the nations. Those who lug wooden idols around and pray to gods that cannot save — they are without understanding.
45.21: State your case. Bring your evidence. Let the nations put their heads together. Who announced this beforehand? Who proclaimed it long ago? Was it not I, Yahweh? There is no other god beside me — a God who is both righteous and saving. No one else.
45.22: Turn to me — be saved, every corner of the earth. I am God, and there is no other.
45.23: I have sworn by my own name — and the righteous word that left my mouth will not return empty: every knee will bow to me, every tongue will swear its allegiance.
45.24: 'Only in Yahweh,' they will say, 'is there righteousness and power.' Everyone who raged against him will come to him — and find themselves ashamed.
45.25: In Yahweh — in Yahweh alone — all the descendants of Israel will be made right, and they will boast in him.

46.1: Bel falls to his knees; Nebo crumbles. Their images are strapped onto animals, loaded onto oxen — the gods you carry have become a load too heavy for exhausted beasts.
46.2: They collapse together — gods going down with the rest. They cannot save the very load they are. They too go into captivity.
46.3: Listen to me, house of Jacob — all the remaining ones of the house of Israel — you whom I have been carrying since before you were born, lifting since the womb,
46.4: even when you are old — I am the one. Even when your hair is white, I will carry you. I made you; I will carry you. I will hold you up and bring you safe through.
46.5: Who is my equal? Who can you put beside me? Name one who compares.
46.6: They pour out gold from their pouches, weigh silver on the scale, and hire a goldsmith to hammer it into a god — then fall flat on their faces to worship it.
46.7: They heave it up on their shoulders, lug it into the house, set it down — and there it stays. It cannot take a single step. Cry out to it — silence. It cannot deliver anyone from anything.
46.8: Take note of this. Hold it firm. Let it come back to you, every one of you who has turned away.
46.9: Remember what happened long before — I am God and there is no other. I am God, and nothing is like me —
46.10: I declared the end before the beginning even happened. I announced things not yet done, from ancient times. My purpose will stand. I will do everything I intend.
46.11: I called a bird of prey from the east — the man I have chosen, from a distant land. I said it — I will bring it to pass. I purposed it — I will carry it out.
46.12: Listen to me, you hardened ones — you who have stayed far from righteousness:
46.13: I am bringing my righteous purpose close — it is not distant. My salvation will not dawdle. I am planting salvation in Zion, my glory in Israel.

47.1:
Come down — sit in the dust,
O virgin daughter of Babylon.
Sit on the ground, with no throne —
O daughter of the Chaldeans.
You will never again be called
tender and delicate.
47.2:
Seize the millstones and grind your own grain.
Pull off your veil.
Strip off your fine robe.
Bare your legs — wade through the rivers.
47.3:
Your nakedness will be exposed;
your shame will be seen.
I will take vengeance.
I will spare no one.
47.4:
(Our Redeemer —
Yahweh of hosts is his name —
the Holy One of Israel.)
47.5:
Sit in silence.
Go into the dark,
O daughter of the Chaldeans.
Never again will anyone call you
the mistress of kingdoms.
47.6:
I was angry with my people.
I gave away my own inheritance —
handed them to you.
But you showed them no mercy.
On the old and frail
you laid your heaviest yoke.
47.7: You said, 'I will be mistress forever.' So you never gave a thought to any of this — never considered how it might end.
47.8:
So hear this now,
you pleasure-drunk one,
you who sit so secure —
you who say in your heart,
'I am, and there is no one but me.
I will never be a widow.
I will never lose my children.'
47.9:
Both of these
shall come on you in a single moment —
in one day:
loss of children and widowhood,
coming upon you in full force —
for all your spells,
for all the power of your enchantments.
47.10:
You felt safe in your wickedness.
You told yourself: 'No one can see me.'
Your cleverness and your knowledge
corrupted you —
and you said in your heart,
'I am, and there is no one else.'
47.11:
But disaster will come upon you —
no spell can drive it away.
Calamity will fall on you —
you will not know how to buy it off.
Ruin will come on you suddenly,
out of nowhere.
47.12:
Stand by your spells then —
all those enchantments
you have practiced since you were young.
Perhaps they will do you some good.
Perhaps they will actually frighten someone.
47.13:
You have worn yourself out
with all your advisers.
Let them step up and rescue you —
the sky-dividers,
the star-gazers,
the ones who, month by month,
claim to know what is coming.
47.14:
Look at them — they are stubble.
The fire will consume them.
They cannot save themselves
from the power of the flames —
no coal left for warmth,
no fire to huddle around.
47.15:
That is what becomes of them —
all those you did business with,
your trading partners since you were young.
Each one turns and goes his own way.
No one saves you. No one.

48.1: Hear this, house of Jacob — you who bear the name of Israel, who came from the line of Judah — you who invoke the name of Yahweh and call on the God of Israel, but not in truth, not from the heart.
48.2: You call yourselves people of the holy city. You claim to lean on the God of Israel — whose name is Yahweh of hosts.
48.3: Long ago I announced the former things. They came out of my mouth; I proclaimed them. Then I acted — suddenly — and they happened.
48.4: Because I knew you — how stubborn you are. Your neck is an iron cable. Your forehead is bronze.
48.5: That is why I told you everything beforehand — before it happened, I proclaimed it — so you could never say, 'My idol made all this happen. My carved image, my cast metal god, gave the orders.'
48.6: You have heard it. Now see it. Will you not say so? From this moment I am announcing new things — things hidden, things you have never known.
48.7: They are being created right now — not long ago. Before today you had never heard of them. I will not let you say, 'I knew this all along.'
48.8: You never heard; you never knew; from the beginning your ear was shut. I knew it — I knew how treacherous you would be. You were called a rebel even before you came into the world.
48.9: For the sake of my own name I hold my anger back. For the sake of my own reputation I restrain myself — so that I do not wipe you out.
48.10: Look — I have refined you, but not like silver. I have tested you in the furnace of affliction.
48.11: For my own sake — for my own sake — I act. How could I let my name be dragged through the mud? I will not give my glory to anyone else.
48.12: Listen, Jacob — Israel, whom I called — listen to me. I am the one. I am the first. I am the last.
48.13: This hand laid the foundation of the earth. This right hand spread out the heavens. When I call out to them, they stand at attention — every one of them.
48.14: Gather and listen, all of you. Which of those gods declared any of this? Yahweh loves Cyrus — he will carry out Yahweh's will against Babylon, and his arm will strike the Chaldeans.
48.15: I — I myself — have spoken it. I called him. I brought him. And he will succeed in everything I sent him to do.
48.16: Come close and hear this: I never spoke from hiding — from the moment things were set in motion, I was there. And now the Lord Yahweh has sent me, and his Spirit.
48.17: Yahweh says this — your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: 'I am Yahweh your God. I teach you what truly benefits you. I lead you in the way you should walk.'
48.18: If only you had listened to my commandments — then your peace would be flowing like a river, your righteousness surging like the ocean's waves;
48.19: your children would be as numberless as grains of sand; their name would never be cut off, never erased from my sight.
48.20: Go out from Babylon! Flee from Chaldea! Shout it with joy — proclaim it — send the word to the farthest corner of the earth: 'Yahweh has redeemed his servant Jacob!'
48.21: They were not thirsty when he led them through the desert. He made water come from the rock — he cracked it open and it poured out.
48.22: There is no peace for the wicked — Yahweh says it.

49.1:
Listen to me, all you distant shores.
Pay attention, peoples from the ends of the earth.
Before I was born, Yahweh called me.
Before I left my mother's womb, he spoke my name.
49.2:
He shaped my mouth into a sharp sword
and hid me in the shadow of his hand.
He made me a polished arrow
and kept me hidden in his quiver.
49.3:
He said to me:
'You are my servant —
Israel, the one through whom I will show my glory.'
49.4:
But I said: I have worn myself out for nothing.
I have exhausted my strength on emptiness and chaos.
And yet — my vindication rests with Yahweh.
My reward is in my God's hands.
49.5:
And now Yahweh speaks —
he who shaped me in the womb to be his servant,
to bring Jacob back to him,
to gather Israel to him.
For I am honored in Yahweh's eyes.
My God has become my strength.
49.6:
He says:
'It is not enough for you to restore the tribes of Jacob
and bring back those of Israel I preserved.
I will make you a light for all the nations —
so that my salvation reaches to the very ends of the earth.'
49.7:
Here is what Yahweh says —
Redeemer of Israel, the Holy One:
To the one who is despised by everyone,
abhorred by the nation,
the servant under rulers' authority —
'Kings will see you and stand to their feet.
Princes will bow to the ground.
Because Yahweh — the faithful one,
Israel's Holy One — has chosen you.'
49.8:
Here is what Yahweh says:
'When the moment of favor arrived, I answered you.
On the day of deliverance, I came to your aid.
I will watch over you.
I will make you a covenant for the peoples —
to resettle the land,
to give back the desolate inheritances,'
49.9:
'to say to the prisoners: Come out!
to say to those in darkness: Step into the light!
They will find pasture along every road;
the bare hilltops will become their grazing ground.'
49.10:
They will never hunger.
They will never thirst.
The scorching wind and the blazing sun will not touch them —
for he who loves them will lead them
and guide them to flowing springs.
49.11:
I will level my mountains into roads.
Every path will be raised and made ready.
49.12:
Look — they are coming from far away.
Some from the north, some from the west,
others from the distant land of Sinim.
49.13:
Sing for joy, heavens!
Rejoice, earth!
Break into song, mountains!
For Yahweh has comforted his people —
he will have mercy on those who suffer.
49.14:
But Zion said:
'Yahweh has abandoned me.
My Lord has forgotten me.'
49.15:
Can a mother forget the infant at her breast —
feel no love for the child she bore?
Even if she could forget —
I will never forget you.
49.16:
Look — I have carved your name into my palms.
Your walls are always before my eyes.
49.17:
Your rebuilders are rushing toward you.
Those who tore you apart and left you in ruins — they will be gone.
49.18:
Lift your eyes. Look around.
They are gathering — coming to you from every direction.
As I live, declares Yahweh —
you will wear them all like a bride's jewels,
you will bind them on and they will adorn you.
49.19:
Your ruined places, your empty land, your devastated ground —
they will be overcrowded with people.
Those who consumed you will be driven away, far from you.
49.20:
The children you thought you would never have
will say in your very hearing:
'This place is too small for us.
Make room — we need more space to live.'
49.21:
Then you will say to yourself:
Who gave me all these children?
I was bereaved. Barren. Exiled. Cast away.
Who raised them up?
I was left completely alone —
where did all these people come from?
49.22:
Here is what Lord Yahweh says:
'I will raise my hand toward the nations.
I will hoist my signal banner for the peoples to see.
They will bring your sons in their arms.
They will carry your daughters on their shoulders.'
49.23:
Kings will serve as your guardians.
Queens will nurse you back to strength.
They will bow before you — faces in the dust —
they will lick the ground at your feet.
Then you will know: I am Yahweh.
No one who waits for me will be put to shame.
49.24:
Can anyone snatch what a warrior has seized?
Can a tyrant's prisoner ever go free?
49.25:
But here is what Yahweh says:
Yes — even what the warrior seizes will be taken back.
Even the tyrant's prisoners will go free.
For I will fight whoever fights against you.
I will rescue your children.
49.26:
I will turn your oppressors against each other —
they will devour their own flesh
and be drunk on their own blood as though on wine.
Then everyone alive will know:
I am Yahweh — your Savior,
your Redeemer,
the Mighty One of Jacob.

50.1:
Here is what Yahweh says:
'Where is the divorce certificate
by which I sent your mother away?
To what creditor did I sell you?
Look honestly:
You were sold because of your own guilt.
Your mother was sent away because of your rebellions.'
50.2:
Why was there no one when I came?
Why did no one answer when I called?
Is my arm too short to save you?
Do I not have the power to deliver?
I rebuke the sea and it dries up.
I turn rivers into desert.
The fish rot in the waterless riverbed —
they die of thirst.
50.3:
I dress the sky in blackness
and make sackcloth the covering of the heavens.
50.4:
Lord Yahweh has given me the tongue of a disciple —
so I know how to speak a sustaining word to the exhausted.
Morning by morning he wakes me.
He opens my ear
the way a master opens his student's ear —
to truly hear.
50.5:
Lord Yahweh opened my ear.
I did not resist.
I did not pull away.
50.6:
I offered my back to those who beat me.
I gave my cheeks to those who tore out my beard.
I did not turn my face away from shame and spitting.
50.7:
But Lord Yahweh is helping me.
That is why I have not been disgraced.
That is why I set my face like a flint —
hard, unmovable.
I know I will not be put to shame.
50.8:
The one who will vindicate me is close at hand.
Who is going to bring a charge against me?
Let us face each other.
Who is my adversary?
Let him come forward.
50.9:
Look — Lord Yahweh is my helper.
Who can condemn me?
All my opponents will wear out like an old coat.
The moth will eat them up.
50.10:
Who among you fears Yahweh
and truly listens to the voice of his servant?
If you walk in deep darkness
and have no light at all —
trust in the name of Yahweh.
Lean on your God.
50.11:
But all of you who strike your own fire —
who light your own torches to see by —
go ahead: walk by the light you made for yourselves.
This is what you will get from my hand:
you will lie down in agony.

51.1:
Listen to me —
you who run after righteousness,
you who seek Yahweh:
Look at the rock from which you were cut.
Look at the quarry from which you were dug.
51.2:
Look to your father Abraham.
Look to Sarah who bore you.
He was only one man when I called him —
but I blessed him
and he became many.
51.3:
For Yahweh will comfort Zion —
he will comfort every place that lies in ruins.
He will make her wilderness like Eden,
her desert like the garden of Yahweh.
Joy and gladness will live there.
Thanksgiving. The sound of singing.
51.4:
Listen to me, my people.
Give me your attention, my nation.
My instruction will go out from me.
My justice will become a light for all peoples.
51.5:
My vindication is close.
My deliverance has gone out.
My arm will bring justice to the nations.
The distant shores are waiting for me.
They are hoping in my strength.
51.6:
Lift your eyes to the heavens.
Look down at the earth.
The heavens will dissolve like smoke.
The earth will wear thin like an old coat.
Everyone on it will die the same way.
But my salvation will stand forever.
My vindication will never be undone.
51.7:
Listen to me —
you who know what is right,
you people who carry my instruction in your hearts.
Do not be afraid of human contempt.
Do not be shattered by their mockery.
51.8:
The moth will consume them like a worn-out coat.
The worm will eat them like wool.
But my vindication will stand forever.
My salvation — through every generation.
51.9:
Awake! Awake!
Arm of Yahweh — put on your strength!
Wake up as you did in the days long past,
in the generations of old.
Was it not you who split Rahab apart
and ran through the dragon of the deep?
51.10:
Was it not you who dried up the sea —
the waters of the vast deep —
who turned the ocean floor into a road
for the redeemed to walk across?
51.11:
The ransomed of Yahweh will come home.
They will arrive in Zion singing,
everlasting joy crowning their heads.
Gladness and joy will surround them.
Sorrow and grief will run away.
51.12:
I — I am the one who comforts you.
So who are you to be afraid of a man who will die,
of a human being made like grass?
51.13:
You have forgotten Yahweh your Maker —
the one who stretched out the heavens
and laid the foundations of the earth.
You cower all day long in the face of the oppressor's rage,
as though he is about to destroy you.
But where is that fury now?
51.14:
The bent-down prisoner will soon be freed.
He will not die in a pit.
His bread will not fail.
51.15:
For I am Yahweh your God —
the one who stirs the sea until its waves crash and thunder.
Yahweh of armies — that is my name.
51.16:
I have placed my words in your mouth.
I have hidden you in the shadow of my hand —
I, who stretched out the heavens,
who laid the foundations of the earth,
who say to Zion:
'You are my people.'
51.17:
Wake up! Wake up!
Get to your feet, Jerusalem!
You have drunk from Yahweh's hand
the cup of his fury —
you have drained the cup of staggering
down to the very last drop.
51.18:
Of all the children she bore, none steps forward to guide her.
Of all the children she raised, none takes her by the hand.
51.19:
Two disasters have overtaken you.
Who will mourn with you?
Devastation and ruin. Famine and the sword.
Who is left to bring you comfort?
51.20:
Your sons have collapsed.
They lie sprawled at every street corner
like a deer caught in a net.
They are saturated with Yahweh's fury —
the full weight of your God's rebuke.
51.21:
So listen to this —
you who are crushed,
drunk with grief and not with wine:
51.22:
Here is what your Lord says —
Yahweh your God,
the one who fights for his people:
'Look — I am taking that staggering cup from your hand.
You will never drink from the bowl of my fury again.'
51.23:
'I will give it instead to those who tormented you —
those who told you: Get down, so we can walk over you.
You made your back into pavement,
you lay flat like a street for them to march across.
That is finished.'

52.1:
Wake up! Wake up, Zion —
put on your strength!
Dress yourself in your finest, Jerusalem, the holy city!
The uncircumcised and the unclean
will never walk your streets again.
52.2:
Shake the dust from yourself — rise up, Jerusalem!
Take your throne!
Free yourself from the chains around your neck,
captive daughter of Zion.
52.3:
For here is what Yahweh says:
'You went into slavery for nothing —
no price was paid for you.
And no price will be required to set you free.'
52.4:
For here is what the Sovereign Yahweh says:
'My people went down to Egypt in the beginning —
they went as strangers seeking refuge.
Then Assyria crushed them, for no reason at all.'
52.5:
'So what do I gain from this?' declares Yahweh.
'My people are taken away without payment.
Their rulers strut and mock,' declares Yahweh,
'and day after day my name is treated with contempt.'
52.6:
'Therefore my people will know who I am.
On that day they will recognise the one who speaks:
Here I am.'
52.7:
How beautiful on the mountains
are the feet of the herald who brings good news —
who announces peace,
who proclaims what is good,
who brings the news of rescue
and says to Zion: Your God is King!
52.8:
Listen — your watchmen are shouting together!
They break into joyful singing —
for with their own eyes, right in front of them,
they see Yahweh returning to Zion.
52.10:
Yahweh has rolled back his sleeve —
his holy arm bare before every nation.
Every corner of the earth will see
what our God does to save.
52.11:
Go! Leave that place!
Touch nothing unclean!
Come out from among them —
purify yourselves, you who carry the holy vessels of Yahweh.
52.12:
But you will not flee this time.
No desperate scramble out the back.
For Yahweh goes ahead of you —
and the God of Israel guards your rear.
52.13:
Watch — my Servant will understand what he is doing.
He will be lifted up, raised high,
exalted above all.
52.14:
Just as many were horrified when they saw him —
his face so disfigured it no longer looked human,
his form altered beyond all recognition —
52.15:
— so he will astonish and consecrate many nations.
Kings will be struck silent at the sight.
For they will see what no one told them.
They will grasp what no one ever announced.

53.1:
Who actually believed what we told them?
Who saw the LORD's power revealed in this?
53.2:
Before the LORD's eyes he grew up
like a sapling pushing through cracked desert ground —
nothing impressive about his appearance,
nothing that would make us want to look at him,
nothing beautiful that would draw our desire.
53.3:
He was despised — pushed away by everyone.
A man who knew what pain was,
well acquainted with grief.
We turned our faces from him as if ashamed.
He was despised, and we paid him no regard.
53.4:
And yet — he was the one who carried what was ours:
our sicknesses, our pain.
We assumed he was being punished by God,
struck down, suffering for his own wrongs.
53.5:
But he was pierced through for our rebellions —
crushed for the guilt we had accumulated.
The discipline that makes us whole fell on him.
By his wounds, we are healed.
53.6:
We all wandered off like sheep —
every one of us turning down our own path.
And Yahweh placed on him
the guilt of all of us.
53.7:
He was pressed under oppression and affliction,
yet he never opened his mouth.
Like a lamb being led to the slaughter —
like a sheep that goes silent before the shearers —
he did not open his mouth.
53.8:
Through a corrupt trial he was swept away.
Who paused to consider what it meant —
that he was cut off from among the living,
struck down for the sins of my people?
53.9:
He was assigned a grave among the wicked —
yet his burial was with the rich.
Not because of any violence he had done;
not a word of deceit had ever crossed his lips.
53.10:
Yet Yahweh's purpose was to crush him, to make him suffer.
When his life is offered as the atoning sacrifice,
he will see his descendants — he will live long —
and Yahweh's purpose will be accomplished through him.
53.11:
When the anguish of his life is past,
he will see what it accomplished — and be satisfied.
By what he knows, my servant, the righteous one,
will make many righteous —
for he will carry their guilt.
53.12:
Therefore I will give him his share among the mighty
and let him divide the plunder with the strong —
because he poured out his life in death,
because he was counted among the rebels,
because he carried the sin of many,
and still speaks up for those who rebel.

54.1:
Sing, barren woman — you who never gave birth!
Break into joyful singing, cry out loud —
you who never knew labor's pain!
For the desolate woman will have more children
than the woman who has a husband —
declares Yahweh.
54.2:
Make room — stretch your tent wider.
Let the canopy of your home spread out.
Don't hold back.
Lengthen the ropes, drive the pegs deeper —
you are going to need the space.
54.3:
For you will expand — right and left, in every direction.
Your children will claim nations as their own
and repopulate cities long abandoned.
54.4:
Do not be afraid — you will not be humiliated.
Do not cringe — you will not be disgraced.
You will forget the shame of your younger years;
the reproach of your widowhood will be forgotten.
54.5:
For the one who made you is your husband —
Yahweh of armies is his name —
and your Kinsman-Redeemer is the Holy One of Israel,
the God of the whole earth — that is who he is.
54.6:
For Yahweh has called you back —
like a wife who was abandoned and heartbroken,
like a wife taken in the years of youth and then sent away.
Your God says this.
54.7:
For one brief, terrible moment I left you.
But with overflowing compassion I am bringing you home.
54.8:
In a surge of anger I turned my face away —
just for a moment.
But with love that will never run out,
I will have compassion on you —
declares Yahweh, your Redeemer.
54.9:
To me, this is the same oath I made in Noah's day:
I swore then that the floodwaters would never again
cover the earth.
Now I swear:
I will never be angry with you again.
I will never rebuke you again.
54.10:
Mountains may crumble.
Hills may fall.
But my covenant loyalty will not leave you.
My covenant of peace will not be shaken.
This is what Yahweh says —
the one who has compassion on you.
54.11:
O city battered and storm-driven, without comfort —
listen:
I will embed your stones in brilliant color;
I will lay your foundations with sapphires.
54.12:
Your towers will be set with rubies.
Your gates will be crystal.
Every wall around you — precious stones.
54.13:
Every one of your children will be taught by Yahweh himself —
and their peace will be profound.
54.14:
Righteousness will be your foundation.
Oppression will be far from you — nothing to fear.
Terror will not come near you.
54.15:
If anyone comes to attack you, it will not be because of me.
Whoever assembles against you will fall before you.
54.16:
I am the one who made the blacksmith
who fans the coals and forges the weapon.
And I am the one who made the destroyer who lays waste.
Even the weapons arrayed against you come from my workshop.
54.17:
No weapon forged against you will succeed.
Every voice raised against you in court — you will answer it.
This is the inheritance of Yahweh's servants:
their righteousness comes from me.
— declares Yahweh.

55.1:
Come — come to the water,
every one who thirsts!
You with no money — come!
Buy and eat!
Come — buy wine and milk
without money, without cost.
55.2:
Why trade your silver for what is not bread —
your wages for what leaves you empty?
Listen — listen to me.
Eat what is good.
Let your whole self delight in the richest feast.
55.3:
Lean in and come to me.
Listen — and you will live.
I will make an everlasting covenant with you:
all the sure and steadfast love I showed David —
that is what I am offering you now.
55.4: I made David a witness to the nations — a prince and commander set over the peoples.
55.5: You will call a nation you never knew, and nations that never knew you will come running — because of Yahweh your God, because of the Holy One of Israel, who has crowned you with honor.
55.6:
Seek Yahweh while he may be found.
Call on him while he is still near.
55.7:
Let the wicked man leave his road.
Let the unrighteous man abandon his schemes.
Let him return to Yahweh — who will have compassion on him —
to our God, who pardons with open hands.
55.8:
My thoughts are not your thoughts.
My ways are not your ways —
Yahweh declares it.
55.9:
As the heavens tower over the earth,
so my ways tower over your ways —
my thoughts over your thoughts.
55.10:
As the rain and the snow fall from the sky
and do not go back up
until they have watered the earth —
until it has budded and sprouted,
until it gives seed to the farmer
and bread to the hungry —
55.11:
so my word — going out of my mouth —
will not return to me empty.
It will accomplish what I intend.
It will succeed in what I send it to do.
55.12:
You will leave with joy.
You will be led out in peace.
The mountains and hills will break into singing before you,
and all the trees of the field will clap their hands.
55.13:
Where thorns grew — cypress will rise.
Where briers spread — myrtle will bloom.
It will be a name for Yahweh,
an everlasting sign that will never be cut off.

56.1: Yahweh says this: Keep justice. Do what is right. My salvation is about to arrive — my righteousness is on the verge of being revealed.
56.2: Blessed is the one who does this — the person who holds to it firm: who keeps the sabbath and does not violate it, who keeps his hands back from every evil.
56.3: The foreigner who has joined himself to Yahweh should not say, 'Yahweh will surely cut me off from his people.' The eunuch should not say, 'I am a dry and withered tree.'
56.4: For Yahweh says this: To the eunuchs who keep my sabbaths, who choose what I delight in and hold fast to my covenant —
56.5: I will give them — within my house, within my walls — a place and a name better than sons and daughters. I will give them an everlasting name that will never be cut off.
56.6: And the foreigners who join themselves to Yahweh — to serve him, to love the name of Yahweh, to be his servants — everyone who keeps the sabbath and does not profane it, who holds fast my covenant —
56.7: I will bring them to my holy mountain and fill them with joy in my house of prayer. Their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be welcomed on my altar. My house will be called a house of prayer for all peoples.
56.8: The Lord Yahweh — who gathers the scattered of Israel — says: I will gather still more to him, beyond those already brought in.
56.9: Come, all you wild beasts of the field — come and feed. Come, all you beasts of the forest.
56.10: His watchmen are all blind. None of them understand. They are all silent dogs — they cannot bark. They dream, they lounge, they love to sleep.
56.11: The dogs are insatiable — never enough. These shepherds have no understanding. Every one of them has gone his own way, chasing his own profit from his own direction.
56.12: Come, they say — let us get wine. Let us fill ourselves with beer. And tomorrow? Tomorrow will be just like today. Even better.

57.1: The righteous perish — and no one gives it a thought. Men of faithful love are taken away, and no one understands. The righteous man is being removed from the evil that is coming;
57.2: he enters into peace — he rests in his resting place — each one who walked in uprightness.
57.3: But you — come here, children of the sorceress, seed of the adulterer and the harlot.
57.4: Who is it you are mocking? At whom do you stretch your mouth wide and stick out your tongue? You are children of rebellion — offspring of deceit.
57.5: You who inflame yourselves with idols beneath every leafy tree — who slaughter children in the ravines, under the crevices of the rocks?
57.6:
